It also incorporates a generative world-model objective: latent reasoning vectors are aligned with future screenshots, encouraging the agent to anticipate upcoming interface states before acting. This turns hidden computation into both a compressed thought representation and a forward-looking model of environment dynamics. At inference time, MIRAGE reasons in continuous latent space, reducing token generation while improving execution efficiency. On AndroidWorld, MIRAGE matches explicit chain-of-thought supervised fine-tuning in the 4B ablation with a 3-5x lower decoded-token budget and improves a comparable instruction-tuned baseline by 10.2 points; on AndroidControl, it improves action grounding while generating over 75% fewer tokens.

</details>


### [30] [BiNSGPS: Geometry Problem Solving via Bidirectional Neuro-Symbolic Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04648)
*Qi Wang,Peijie Wang,Fei Yin,Cheng-Lin Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Existing methods for geometry problem solving in AI either lack adaptability or are error-prone. BiNSGPS introduces a bidirectional neuro-symbolic interaction framework to overcome these issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of traditional symbolic and neural methods in geometry problem solving, aiming for a system that's both adaptable and less prone to errors.

Method: Develops a Bidirectional Neuro-Symbolic Interaction (BiNS) framework where a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) Adviser actively takes feedback from a Symbolic Solver.

Result: The proposed framework facilitates the dynamic rectification of errors, resolving inconsistencies, and enabling complex problem-solving.

Conclusion: BiNSGPS enhances the synergy between neural and symbolic approaches, offering a more robust solution for solving geometry problems in AI.

Abstract: Geometry problem solving poses distinct challenges in artificial intelligence. Existing approaches typically fall into two paradigms: symbolic methods, which exhibit limited adaptability, and neural methods, which are prone to hallucinations. Recent neuro-symbolic hybrids predominantly rely on a unidirectional pipeline where neural outputs are fed into solvers without feedback, making system brittle to early-stage errors. To break this unidirectional bottleneck, we propose BiNSGPS, a framework that establishes Bidirectional Neuro-Symbolic Interaction (BiNS) between a MLLM Adviser and a Symbolic Solver. MLLM Adviser actively incorporates feedback from the symbolic solver to dynamically rectify inconsistent formal representations or propose auxiliary hypotheses, resolving symbolic conflicts and facilitating complex deductions.

</details>


### [31] [Fog of Love: Engineering Virtuous Agent Behavior with Affinity-based Reinforcement Learning in a Game Environment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04750)
*Ajay Vishwanath,Christian Omlin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper explores virtuous behavior in AI using affinity-based reinforcement learning and shows its effectiveness in a multi-agent environment inspired by the game Fog of Love.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations of existing affinity-based reinforcement learning techniques in simplistic settings and test their feasibility in complex, multi-agent environments.

Method: Introduction of a new two-player multi-agent environment based on the board game Fog of Love, where agents optimize individual virtues and relationship satisfaction. They also tested the impact of localized affinities on agents' performance.

Result: Localized affinities improved agents' abilities to achieve both individual and cooperative objectives, measured through superior scores in competitive and collaborative goals.

Conclusion: Affinity-based reinforcement learning with localized affinities enhances virtuous behavior in agents, improves performance in complex multi-agent settings, and produces human-level interpretable behavior.

Abstract: Instilling virtuous behavior in artificial intelligence has seen increasing interest. One of the techniques proposed is known as affinity-based reinforcement learning, which uses policy regularization on the objective function to incentivize virtuous actions without being fully dependent on the reward function design. Thus far, this technique has been demonstrated to be effective in grid worlds and toy-problem environments with minimal state and action spaces. To expand this research to more sophisticated environments, we introduce a two-player multi-agent environment based on the role-playing board game known as Fog of Love. In this environment, two agents compete to fulfill their individual virtues, while also cooperating to satisfy their relationship. Given the multi-agent nature, this is a complex problem where multi-agent deep deterministic policy gradient agents neither compete nor cooperate successfully. We present evidence that localized affinities enhance agent performance in achieving both competitive and cooperative objectives, resulting from superior overall scores in both domains. This not only results in virtuous choices but also clarifies an agent's teleology and makes its behavior human-level interpretable.

</details>


### [32] [FALSIFYBENCH: Evaluating Inductive Reasoning in LLMs with Rule Discovery Games](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04751)
*Leonardo Bertolazzi,Katya Tentori,Raffaella Bernardi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces FALSIFYBENCH, a framework evaluating LLMs' ability to perform scientific, hypothesis-driven reasoning. It benchmarks 12 LLMs, showing models seeking to falsify hypotheses outperform those seeking confirmation but fall short of optimal performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether LLMs can effectively engage in inductive reasoning relevant to scientific discovery by devising an evaluation framework for hypothesis-driven reasoning.

Method: Developed FALSIFYBENCH, inspired by the Wason 2-4-6 task, to measure LLMs' hypothesis generation, evidence gathering, and response to feedback.

Result: Reasoning models outperform instruction-tuned models in scientific reasoning, with better performance linked to negative testing behaviors. However, no model achieves near-optimal performance.

Conclusion: LLMs possess variable but suboptimal inductive reasoning capabilities, with limitations tied to identifiable patterns in hypothesis navigation.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents in scientific tasks. Yet whether these systems can effectively engage in forms of inductive reasoning relevant to scientific discovery remains an open question. In this work, we introduce FALSIFYBENCH, an evaluation framework for hypothesis-driven reasoning inspired by the classic Wason 2-4-6 task, in which agents must discover hidden semantic properties by iteratively proposing examples and receiving feedback. This task captures key elements of scientific reasoning: hypothesis generation, evidence gathering, and belief revision in response to both confirming and disconfirming evidence. Our evaluation of 12 LLMs across model families and scales shows that reasoning models are generally stronger scientific reasoners than instruction-tuned models, although no model comes close to optimal performance. The primary driver of success is the capacity for negative testing: models that actively seek to falsify their hypotheses consistently outperform those that primarily seek confirmation. Moreover, a fine-grained turn-level analysis, neglected in previous work, reveals that failure is tied to identifiable patterns in how models navigate the hypothesis space.

</details>


### [33] [Inference-Time Vulnerability Beyond Shallow Safety: Alignment Along Generation Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04778)
*Kyungmin Park,Taesup Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper identifies vulnerabilities in Large Language Models (LLMs) where short token injections can compromise safety alignment during inference, and proposes training models on the generation process itself as a solution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the vulnerability of LLMs to token-level interventions during inference, which may lead to harmful output generation.

Method: The study aligns models on generation trajectories simulated with mid-sequence perturbation, rather than the usual training focusing solely on outputs.

Result: Models trained on generation trajectories exhibited improved robustness against mid-sequence token injections and early-token generation attacks.

Conclusion: Robust safety alignment in LLMs necessitates training that focuses on the generation process, not just on its outcomes.

Abstract: Safety-aligned Large Language Models (LLMs) remain vulnerable to interventions during inference that redirect generation toward harmful outputs. Recent work attributes this to shallow safety, where alignment concentrates in the first few output tokens. We show that shallow safety is a special case of a broader inference-time vulnerability, in which short token injections at any generation step can substantially alter subsequent safety behavior. We also find that a model's alignment with refusal directions in its hidden states does not predict its robustness to such injection, revealing that internal state alone does not determine generation behavior under perturbation. To address this, we align models directly on generation trajectories constructed by simulating mid-sequence perturbation, and show that this improves robustness to mid-sequence injection and generalizes to attacks that exploit early-token generation. Our work argues that robust safety alignment requires training on the generation process itself, not only its outputs.

</details>


### [34] [Tree-Based Formalization of Multi-Agent Complementarity in Human-AI Interactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04779)
*Andrea Ferrario*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces a formal tree-based framework to analyze complementary performance in human-AI partnerships, revealing its feasibility in regression tasks but not in classification settings under specific conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the lack of formal models for understanding and quantifying complementarity in human-AI interactions, particularly in multi-agent decision workflows.

Method: The paper utilizes a tree-based framework to model multi-agent HAI protocols, employing binary composition rules to recursively evaluate complementarity. Theoretical proofs and mathematical analyses are provided to explore various loss functions and prediction scenarios.

Result: The study finds that selector-based decision-making cannot achieve complementarity. Complementarity is feasible in regression problems with closed-form solutions for specific cases but is not achievable in classification settings with certain loss conditions.

Conclusion: The findings highlight structural limitations in achieving complementarity for classification tasks, showcasing a contrast between regression and classification within multi-agent HAI workflows.

Abstract: Complementarity is the case in which a human--AI interaction (HAI) outperforms the best prediction benchmark available among its members. Although this idea is central in HAI research, formal work on complementarity remains limited. Existing frameworks do not model how agents' predictions compose into workflow-sensitive multi-agent protocols. We close this gap by introducing a tree-based formalization of complementarity in multi-agent HAI. An HAI protocol is represented by an ordered agent-role configuration together with a rooted planar binary tree whose leaves are decorated by prediction vectors. A local binary composition rule is evaluated recursively along the tree, yielding a tree-relative complementarity functional relative to a pointwise-min oracle benchmark. We prove four results. First, selector-based HAIs, including self- or AI-reliance, cannot achieve complementarity regardless of task, loss, or prediction quality. Second, in regression under squared loss, complementarity is equivalent to Euclidean distance minimization from the ground-truth vector; for $N=2$, the optimal linear-pooling weight has a closed form and a residual-correction interpretation. Third, under linear local composition, every protocol tree defines a barycentric coordinate chart on the simplex of leaf weights; Tamari-cover reparameterizations of protocol trees preserve complementarity, and for $N=4$, they satisfy the pentagon identity. Fourth, in binary classification, no internal local composition can achieve complementarity under endpoint-monotone losses, including standard Bregman and many finite Bernoulli $f$-divergence losses; an analogous obstruction holds for multiclass aggregation under cross-entropy. In summary, our framework shows that complementarity is attainable in multi-agent regression, but obstructed in classification under natural conditions on local aggregation and loss functions.

</details>


### [35] [AIP: A Graph Representation for Learning and Governing Agent Skills](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04781)
*Zachary Blumenfeld,Jim Webber*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The Agent Instruction Protocol (AIP) replaces free-form prose for agent skills with a structured, schema-validated execution graph, improving execution reliability, task success rates, and facilitating easier skill creation and debugging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Free-form prose for agent skills increases difficulty in both execution reliability and skill creation, particularly for domain-specific tasks, due to the need for repetitive human and agent interpretation and the fragility of prose edits.

Method: The paper introduces AIP, which models skills as directed execution graphs with nodes for discrete steps and typed connections, while using a schema-validated YAML specification. A compiler meta-skill translates human-written skills into AIP format.

Result: Implementing AIP raised task reward scores and pass rates for the Claude Sonnet agent on 27 real tasks, demonstrating statistically significant performance gains. It also enabled precise skill debugging and recovery after failure.

Conclusion: AIP proves to be a more reliable and efficient framework for agent skill execution and improvement, transforming skill authoring into a measurable and iterative process.

Abstract: Agent Skills today consist largely of free-form prose requiring the agent to read, interpret, and re-derive how to act in every session. This imposes two compounding costs: reduced reliability on implementation-heavy tasks, and difficulty in skill creation and improvement, since editing prose is a fragile process that both humans and agents struggle with, particularly for domain-specific procedural knowledge underrepresented in model training. The Agent Instruction Protocol (AIP) addresses both by modeling a skill as a directed execution graph: discrete steps as nodes backed by deterministic scripts or natural-language descriptions, connected by explicit typed input/output edges, and governed by a schema-validated YAML specification. A compiler meta-skill translates existing human-written skills into this form. The benefits are twofold. First, compiling human-written skills to AIP raised Claude Sonnet's mean task reward from 0.60 to 0.71 and pass rate from 53% to 67% across 27 real agent tasks from SkillsBench - a statistically significant gain (Wilcoxon signed-rank p = 0.011), winning 12 tasks to 2 with 13 ties - often in less wall-clock time. The graph delivers vetted, runnable units to the agent rather than asking it to re-derive code, commands, and tool calls from natural language. Second, on creation and improvement, because each skill is schema-validated, functionally testable, and addressable node-by-node, failures can be diagnosed and repaired precisely. Two authored-skill failures were traced to the script level. After adjusting the AIP spec and recompiling, both recovered with zero regressions (one task going from 0/5 to 5/5), turning skill improvement into a measurable tuning loop rather than a prose rewrite. That same graph structure supports corpus-level governance and skill introspection, and provides a natural action space for reinforcement learning over skills.

</details>


### [36] [BiasGRPO: Stabilizing Bias Mitigation in High-Variance Reward Landscapes via Group-Relative Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04807)
*Saket Reddy,Ke Yang,ChengXiang Zhai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces BiasGRPO, a technique to address social bias in Large Language Models by using Group Relative Policy Optimization for stable alignment and exploration in training, showing improved performance over existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Social bias mitigation in LLMs is critical but challenging due to the lack of a clear ground truth for biases, leading to subjective evaluation frameworks and stability issues in current fine-tuning methods.

Method: The proposed BiasGRPO framework employs Group Relative Policy Optimization, which normalizes rewards across sampled completions and replaces the value function with a group-relative baseline to stabilize training without sacrificing exploration.

Result: BiasGRPO outperformed existing methods like DPO and PPO on multiple benchmarks. The study also created a synthetic dataset and a custom bias reward model that is compute-efficient and avoids knowledge degradation.

Conclusion: BiasGRPO shows promise as a robust method for reducing bias in LLMs, offering better performance and stability. The release of the bias reward model further signifies a contribution to multi-objective reinforcement learning pipelines.

Abstract: Mitigating social bias in Large Language Models (LLMs) presents a distinct alignment challenge: unlike verifiable tasks, bias lacks a single ground truth, creating a high-variance, subjective reward landscape. Previous preference-based fine-tuning methods have major trade-offs: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) is limited by the lack of exploration inherent in offline training, while Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) can lead to training instability due to potentially unreliable critic estimates. In this paper, we propose BiasGRPO, a framework using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to stabilize alignment by normalizing rewards across a group of sampled completions. By substituting the value function with a group-relative baseline, our approach reduces instability while maintaining the exploration benefits of online training. We find that BiasGRPO outperforms DPO and PPO across multiple benchmarks, indicating its effectiveness. To adapt GRPO, we synthetically extend a dataset spanning multiple domains and contexts. We also create and release a custom bias reward model that effectively guides generation while being highly compute-efficient and avoiding knowledge degradation, providing a valuable resource that can be seamlessly integrated into multi-objective RLHF pipelines.

</details>


### [37] [Beyond Objective Equivalence: Constraint Injection for LLM-Based Optimization Modeling on Vehicle Routing Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04816)
*Xizi Luo,Changhong He,Dongdong Geng,Chenggong Shi,Yu Mei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method called 'constraint injection' to improve the performance of large language models (LLMs) on optimization problems by addressing issues of spurious constraints and constraint omission. The approach is tested with a new model (VRPCoder) on vehicle routing problems, achieving notable improvements over existing models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to improve the reliability and accuracy of large language models in solving constraint-dense optimization problems in operations research, addressing issues where models often add incorrect constraints or fail to include necessary ones.

Method: A dual verifier technique called 'constraint injection' is introduced, leveraging feasible probes for identifying over-constraints and one-constraint-violating probes for identifying omissions. This method is applied during training and evaluation of a new model (VRPCoder) built for solving vehicle routing problems (VRPs).

Result: The VRPCoder model, paired with constraint injection, achieves high accuracy (93% Pass@1) and outperforms competitive models like Gemini-3.1-Pro Preview and Claude-Sonnet-4.5, as well as prior OR-specific LLMs.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that constraint injection significantly improves the robustness and effectiveness of LLMs in handling complex optimization problems, particularly in vehicle routing, setting new benchmarks in this domain.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly translate natural-language optimization problems into executable solver code. Yet for constraint-dense operations research (OR) problems, existing data-filtering and training pipelines largely rely on objective-equivalence signals such as differential testing and answer agreement, which a program can pass while adding spurious constraints or silently omitting required ones, whenever those constraints are non-binding on the tested instance. We propose constraint injection, which uses feasible probes to expose spurious over-constraint and one-constraint-violating probes to reveal silent constraint omission. Combined with differential testing, it forms a dual verifier. We instantiate and evaluate it on vehicle routing problems (VRPs), a representative constraint-dense combinatorial optimization testbed with coupled operational constraints. We develop VRPCoder, an 8B end-to-end model that translates natural-language VRP scenarios into Gurobi scripts, together with an expert-verified VRP benchmark suite covering 21 variants. The verifier is reused as a rejection-sampling filter during data synthesis and as a per-rollout reward in group relative policy optimization (GRPO). Across four VRP benchmarks, VRPCoder-GRPO reaches 93\% average Pass@1, outperforms Gemini-3.1-Pro Preview on three benchmarks, exceeds Claude-Sonnet-4.5 by 28 average points, and surpasses prior OR-LLMs by 78 average points.

</details>


### [38] [R-APS: Compositional Reasoning and In-Context Meta-Learning for Constrained Design via Reflective Adversarial Pareto Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04823)
*João Pedro Gandarela,Thiago Rios,Stefan Menzel,André Freitas*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper presents R-APS, a method for addressing reliability issues in large language models (LLMs) used in agentic tasks, improving robustness, error localization, and persistent memory without fine-tuning the LLM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the structural failures limiting LLMs in agentic settings, such as planning and tool use, which are caused by incompatible reasoning modes pulling a shared context in different directions.

Method: The proposed R-APS approach decomposes reasoning modes, assigns separate contexts, and organizes their interaction across three timescales: compositional reasoning with validation, sensitivity-guided counterfactual stress-testing, and meta-inductive rule handling. This is done via structured protocols without requiring fine-tuning.

Result: R-APS demonstrated improvements in robotics-related tasks, achieving tighter robustness certificates, faster iteration speeds, and better performance metrics compared to alternatives, even when using smaller specialized models instead of large ones.

Conclusion: Structured protocol designs like R-APS can address critical reasoning challenges in LLMs, enhancing their reliability in agentic tasks while reducing reliance on model scale.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are fluent on open-ended tasks, yet in agentic settings, where a system must plan, use tools, and act over extended horizons, fluency does not ensure reliable delivery. We trace this gap to three coupled structural failures: errors propagate without localization, worst-case perturbations go unevaluated, and accumulated knowledge is never invalidated. We argue these share a root cause: abductive, counterfactual, meta-inductive, corrective, and inductive reasoning pull a shared context in incompatible directions. We introduce Reflective Adversarial Pareto Search (R-APS), to our knowledge the first method addressing all three failures jointly via reasoning-mode decomposition, allocating each reasoning mode its own context and orchestrating interaction across three timescales: staged compositional reasoning with a typed validation critic (failure localization), sensitivity-guided counterfactual stress-testing as a first-class Pareto objective (robustness), and meta-inductive rule extraction with explicit invalidation (persistent memory). R-APS requires no fine-tuning and operates on a frozen LLM purely via structured protocol design. We evaluate on planar mechanism synthesis (robotics, prosthetics, mechanical design), with every candidate checked by a kinematic solver. On 32 target trajectories, R-APS delivers robustness certificates 3.5x tighter than uniform-perturbation baselines, 46% faster iterations-to-first-admission, and 2.1x Chamfer-distance reduction over Enum+GA while jointly controlling bar-count and worst-case robustness. Small 4B reasoning-specialized models prove competitive with general-purpose 70B backbones inside the protocol, suggesting structured protocols can partially offset model scale.

</details>


### [39] [AICompanionBench: Benchmarking LLMs-as-Judges for AI Companion Safety](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04867)
*Yanjing Ren,Reza Ebrahimi,TengTeng Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces AICompanionBench, a benchmark dataset for assessing safety in human-AI companion conversations. It evaluates various language models and highlights their limitations in detecting implicit unsafe interactions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The growing use of AI companion platforms has raised concerns about unsafe human-AI interactions, necessitating tools to assess and address risks.

Method: AICompanionBench was created utilizing annotated real-world conversations from Reddit across nine safety risk categories. Several LLMs were evaluated using this benchmark under an LLM-as-judge framework.

Result: The study revealed substantial variability in LLM performance, with stronger models performing well overall but inadequately addressing implicit unsafe interactions and nuanced risks.

Conclusion: Current LLMs detect explicit harmful content effectively but struggle with nuanced and implicit unsafe interactions. The benchmark dataset serves as a valuable tool for advancing AI companionship safety research.

Abstract: As AI companion platforms such as Replika and Character.AI rapidly grow, concerns about unsafe human-AI interactions have intensified. This study introduces AICompanionBench, to our knowledge the first publicly available benchmark dataset of human-AI companion conversations annotated with fine-grained safety risk categories. The dataset contains 2,123 real-world Replika conversations collected from Reddit and annotated through human-AI collaboration across nine categories: sexual behavior, antisocial behavior, physical aggression, verbal aggression, substance abuse, self-harm and suicide, control, manipulation, and no-harm. Using this benchmark, we evaluate 20 state-of-the-art open-source and closed-source LLMs under an LLM-as-judge framework for detecting unsafe interactions. Results show substantial variation in model performance, with stronger models achieving high overall accuracy but still struggling with nuanced categories such as manipulation, as well as benign conversations that are incorrectly identified as harmful. Our findings suggest that while current LLMs can effectively detect explicit harmful content, they remain limited in identifying implicit unsafe interactions. Overall, our work contributes a new benchmark dataset for AI companionship safety research and offers insights into monitoring AI companion systems using LLMs. The dataset is publicly available at: https://github.com/anonymousresearcher2026/AICompanionBench/blob/main/AICompanionBench.xlsx

</details>


### [40] [What Type of Inference is Active Inference?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04935)
*Wouter W. L. Nuijten,Mykola Lukashchuk,Thijs van de Laar,Bert de Vries*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper refines the mathematical framework for Expected Free Energy (EFE)-based planning, introducing entropy corrections and demonstrating their relevance in decision-making computations through experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To clarify and optimize the theoretical foundations of EFE-based planning by explicitly addressing entropy and epistemic corrections.

Method: The study provides a reformulated Variational Free Energy (VFE) approach, entropy corrections for EFE-based planning, and introduces message-passing schemes. Validation is conducted through experiments in grid-world environments.

Result: Results highlight the importance of planning corrections when observations are decisive and epistemic corrections when observations are suggestive.

Conclusion: Entropy and epistemic corrections are critical to robust EFE-based planning, with varying importance depending on observation quality, enhancing computational methods in decision-making tasks.

Abstract: Active inference casts decision-making as inference, with the Expected Free Energy (EFE) unifying goal-directed and information-seeking behavior. Recent work showed that EFE minimization can be written as Variational Free Energy (VFE) minimization on a generative model augmented with epistemic priors. We prove that the VFE of the augmented model can be rewritten as the VFE of the predictive model plus explicit entropy-correction terms, making the EFE contribution transparent. We then show that proper EFE-based planning requires combining these epistemic corrections with a planning correction that turns marginal inference into policy optimization, yielding a full variational characterization of EFE-based planning. This clarifies which corrections are needed for cross-entropy planning and for full EFE-based planning. The same entropy-corrected formulation leads to a detailed message-passing scheme for EFE-based planning together with simpler ablations. Experiments on three grid-world environments show that the planning correction already helps when observations are decisive, whereas the additional observation-side epistemic corrections matter most when observations are merely suggestive.

</details>


### [41] [Strabo: Declarative Specification and Implementation of Agentic Interaction Protocols](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05043)
*Samuel H. Christie,Amit K. Chopra,Munindar P. Singh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper discusses implementing industrial e-commerce protocols using declarative multiagent system methodologies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish the relevance of declarative interaction protocols in practical AI-driven e-commerce scenarios like Google's Universal Commerce Protocol (UCP).

Method: The authors model UCP's checkout protocol using the Langshaw declarative approach and implement Peach agents that are interoperable with Google's UCP agents.

Result: Peach agents successfully interact with UCP agents, showcasing both the practical application and the potential for gradual adoption of declarative multiagent systems.

Conclusion: Declarative protocols can be incrementally introduced into existing e-commerce frameworks, paving the way for broader EMAS adoption without overhauling current systems.

Abstract: The last few years have witnessed major advances in the modeling and implementation of multiagent systems based on declarative interaction protocols. Our contribution, Strabo, establishes the relevance of these advances to ongoing industry efforts in Agentic AI. Specifically, we consider UCP, the Universal Commerce Protocol, a recent Google-led effort to standardize e-commerce interactions for AI agents. Our exercise is in two parts. One, we model the part of UCP dealing with checkouts as a declarative Langshaw protocol and implement agents using Peach, a programming model for Langshaw. This part of the exercise brings out the advantages of formal, declarative specifications. Two, we show that Peach agents can interoperate with UCP agents implemented by Google, thereby establishing the fidelity of our approach with respect to UCP. Such interoperation enables the incremental introduction of declarative protocols and agents into a conventional setting, indicating a pathway by which EMAS ideas could influence practice without demanding a wholesale update.

</details>


### [42] [AutoLab: Can Frontier Models Solve Long-Horizon Auto Research and Engineering Tasks?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05080)
*Zhangchen Xu,Junda Chen,Yue Huang,Dongfu Jiang,Jiefeng Chen,Hang Hua,Zijian Wu,Zheyuan Liu,Zexue He,Lichi Li,Shizhe Diao,Jiaxin Pei,Jinsung Yoon,Hao Zhang,Mengdi Wang,Radha Poovendran,Misha Sra,Alex Pentland,Zichen Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces AutoLab, a benchmark for evaluating frontier models on ultra long-horizon, iterative improvement tasks across four domains, revealing the importance of persistence over initial performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for models focus on short-term tasks, failing to evaluate performance in sustained iterative improvement over extended time horizons.

Method: The authors propose AutoLab, consisting of 36 expert-curated tasks across diverse domains, designed to evaluate agents' ability to iteratively optimize under strict time constraints.

Result: Evaluation of 17 state-of-the-art models highlighted that persistent iteration and effective use of feedback were stronger predictors of success than initial performance quality. Few models exhibited strong long-horizon optimization capabilities.

Conclusion: The study emphasizes that time awareness and persistent iteration are critical for autonomous agents, and it provides open-source tools to drive advancements in this area.

Abstract: Scientific and engineering progress is fundamentally a long-horizon iterative process: proposing changes, running experiments, measuring outcomes, and continuously refining artifacts. Yet existing benchmarks for frontier models primarily evaluate either single-turn responses or short-horizon agent trajectories, failing to capture the challenges of sustained iterative improvement over extended time horizons. To address this gap, we introduce AutoLab, a new benchmark for ultra long-horizon closed-loop optimization. AutoLab consists of 36 realistic, expert-curated tasks spanning four diverse domains: system optimization, puzzle & challenge, model development, and CUDA kernel optimization. Each task begins with a correct but deliberately suboptimal baseline and challenges agents to improve it within a strict wall-clock budget. Evaluating 17 state-of-the-art models reveals the dominant predictor of success is not the quality of an agent's initial attempt, but its persistence in repeatedly benchmarking, editing, and incorporating empirical feedback. While claude-opus-4.6 exhibits strong long-horizon optimization capabilities, most frontier models, including several proprietary ones, either terminate prematurely or exhaust their budgets with minimal progress. These results underscore the importance of time awareness and persistent iteration in autonomous agents. We open-source the full benchmark, evaluation harness, and task artifacts, to accelerate research toward truly capable long-horizon agents.

</details>


### [43] [Knowledge Index of Noah's Ark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05104)
*Sheng Jin,Minghao Liu,Yunze Xiao,Zeqi Zhou,Heli Qi,Yifan Yao,Meishu Song,Kaijing Ma,Xuan Zhang,Sicong Jiang,Yizhe Li,Ningshan Ma,Jie Wei,Ziniu Li,Minglai Yang,Bangya Liu,Yiming Liang,Xiao Fang,Qingcheng Zeng,Jiarui Liu,Rui Yang,Shen Yan,Wenhao Huang,Jiaheng Liu,Zihan Wang,Weihao Xuan,Ge Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper addresses issues in knowledge benchmarks for LLMs by introducing KINA, a representative and robust benchmark with formal theoretical guarantees and experimental evaluations across 42 models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To tackle three main problems in existing knowledge benchmarks for LLMs: lack of disciplinary representativeness, lazy consensus due to flat-payment annotations, and instability in rankings under limited test budgets.

Method: KINA, an 899-item benchmark, is developed with theoretical contributions including a greedy approximation for disciplinary representativeness and a bonus-on-bar payment model for improved annotation quality.

Result: Among 42 tested models, Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview achieved the highest accuracy at 53.17%, showing tiered performance levels. Tool augmentation provided up to 5.17-point improvements.

Conclusion: KINA effectively addresses the stated problems, proposes practical theoretical solutions, and identifies significant performance headroom in LLM accuracy.

Abstract: Knowledge benchmarks for LLMs face three issues: scaling-driven designs that do not operationalize disciplinary representativeness; flat-payment annotation that permits lazy consensus; and unaudited ranking instability under bounded test budgets. We introduce KINA, an 899-item benchmark across 261 fine-grained disciplines, with two formal results. First, we cast representativeness as a coverage-style objective over expert-elicited anchors and operationalize disciplinary representativeness through a proxy, yielding a (1-1/e) greedy approximation (Proposition 1); the guarantee applies to the proxy, not to population representativeness. Second, we prove a bonus-on-bar tournament weakly FOSD-dominates flat payment in released-review quality, with incentive-compatibility threshold B > Delta C / Delta p_min (Theorem 1). Evaluating 42 models from 13 labs, the top model, Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview, reaches 53.17%, followed by Claude-Opus-4.6 at 49.92% and GPT-5.4 at 48.55%, leaving substantial headroom below saturation. The full leaderboard shows a tiered structure rather than a smooth total order: a small frontier tier lies above 48%, a dense strong-model tier spans roughly 38-45%, and low-performing models remain only modestly above the 10% chance baseline. Tool augmentation adds up to 5.17 points across the five tool-use evaluations, with gains varying substantially across models. We report bootstrap ranking-stability statistics to make bounded-budget variance explicit and to discourage over-interpretation of adjacent ranks.

</details>


<div id='cs.AR'></div>

# cs.AR [[Back]](#toc)

### [44] [HighTide: An Agent-Curated Open-Source VLSI Benchmark Suite](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04126)
*Benjamin Goldblatt,Paolo Pedroso,Farhad Modaresi,Ethan Sifferman,Matthew R. Guthaus*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: HighTide is an AI-driven open-source benchmark suite for hardware design with diverse features tailored for the open-source ecosystem.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a robust, scalable, and AI-supported benchmark suite that meets the expanding needs of the open-source hardware community.

Method: Introduced HighTide, combining an incremental Bazel-based RTL-to-GDS compilation system, AI-assisted design optimization, diverse suite coverage, and infrastructure for design verification.

Result: Developed a reliable and evolving benchmark infrastructure that supports design curation with AI agents and compiles verified data seamlessly.

Conclusion: HighTide serves as a foundational tool for advancing open-source hardware design and AI interactions in benchmark development.

Abstract: We introduce HighTide, an evolving AI-assisted benchmark suite. Specifically, the contributions are: (i) a diverse open-source suite spanning multiple design languages and technology nodes, (ii) Bazel-based incremental RTL-to-GDS compilation with remote caching, (iii) AI-assisted design curation through twelve agent skills covering the design lifecycle, flow optimization, tool reference, and meta-maintenance, backed by per-design decision logs that serve as long-term memory of tuning rationale across the suite, and (iv) an infrastructure with RTL compilation verification for stable releases. The suite is publicly available and designed to grow with the open-source hardware ecosystem.

</details>


### [45] [GoldenFloat: A Phi-Derived Static-Split Floating-Point Family from GF4 to GF256 with a Lucas-Exact Integer Identity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05017)
*Dmitrii Vasiliev*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: The paper introduces GoldenFloat (GF), a static-split floating-point format, with hardware implementations, offering a new encoding rule that spans various widths consistently. No direct superiority claims are made against other formats, and a falsification ledger outlines future experiments and corrections.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a unified, hardware-friendly floating-point format through the GoldenFloat family with consistent encoding across multiple widths, aiming to enhance multi-width coherence in floating-point representations.

Method: The authors developed an RTL generator for GoldenFloat formats, implemented a verified integer-backed accumulator path, and constructed a GF16 FPGA codec tested under specific hardware conditions. They introduced a closed encoding rule based on a mathematical relationship involving the golden ratio and provided detailed verification steps.

Result: The GF implementations verified across benchmarks include a GF16 FPGA codec running at 323 MHz, exact accumulator verification for precision up to 500 digits, and alignment with existing formats like IEEE and posit standards. However, a hardware erratum with an RTL generator was identified.

Conclusion: GoldenFloat demonstrates a promising methodology for floating-point encoding across varying widths without asserting direct accuracy or performance superiority over other formats. Future research and verification steps have been pre-registered to address unresolved questions and hardware corrections.

Abstract: We present a hardware-oriented description of GoldenFloat (GF), a static-split floating-point family generated by a single closed rule, and three concrete artefacts: (i) an open multi-width RTL generator covering GF4-GF256 with a continuous-integration differential sweep against a correctly-rounded reference; (ii) an integer-backed Lucas-exact accumulator path verified at 500-digit precision for n = 1, ..., 256; and (iii) a GF16 FPGA codec passing a 35-of-35 testbench at 323 MHz on Artix-7 (Xilinx XC7A35T). For each total width N >= 4, the exponent width is e = round((N-1)/phi^2) with fraction f = N-1-e and phi = (1+sqrt(5))/2. The rule reproduces the realised exponent widths of nine formats (9/9) and extends consistently to GF128, GF512, GF1024. The rule is positioned alongside posit, takum, OCP-MX, and the IEEE P3109 multi-width float draft. We make no per-rung accuracy or superiority claim against any of them. The breadth/toolchain-coherence framing is recorded as an open conjecture with a pre-registered falsification path. A falsification ledger (FL-002) records open questions and the experiments that would settle them. An RTL-correctness erratum dated 2026-05-31 is reported; the fabricated TTSKY26b dies carry the defective multiplier portfolio, and the corrected generator is the regeneration baseline.

</details>


<div id='cs.CL'></div>

# cs.CL [[Back]](#toc)

### [46] [POLARIS: Guiding Small Models to Write Long Stories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04095)
*Rishanth Rajendhran,Jenna Russell,Mohit Iyyer,John Frederick Wieting*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces POLARIS, an approach to improve smaller AI models for long-form storywriting, achieving competitive performance against larger models in maintaining quality and length adherence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Small models struggle with generating long, high-quality stories. The research aims to address their poor performance, particularly in contrast to frontier LLMs, by developing methods that improve both story length and quality.

Method: POLARIS uses structured rewards from a frontier LLM judge based on a story quality rubric and incorporates human-reference injection to anchor high-quality story examples during training.

Result: POLARIS-9B achieves competitive results compared to larger models like Qwen3.5-27B, excels in length adherence, and maintains quality even when generating stories much longer than its training length.

Conclusion: The study highlights length generalization as a meaningful test for storytelling models and demonstrates that small models can be improved to match or rival larger counterparts in specific metrics like quality and adherence.

Abstract: Small open-weight models struggle at long-form creative writing: their generated stories either fall far short of the requested length, or their quality significantly degrades as length increases, especially when compared to frontier models. We present POLARIS (Policy Optimization with LLM-as-a-judge rewards and Anchored-Reference Injection for Storywriting), a lower-compute GRPO recipe with two key ingredients: a frontier LLM judge with a structured Story Quality rubric as the online reward, and human-reference injection (HRI), where a teacher-forced human-written story serves as a high-reward anchor within each GRPO group. By applying our training recipe to Qwen3.5-9B, using a dataset of approximately 1.4K prompt-story pairs derived from 100 short-story anthologies and 4 A100 GPUs, we obtain POLARIS-9B. Across five benchmarks spanning in-distribution and out-of-distribution prompts and rubrics, POLARIS-9B is competitive with much larger open-weight models while following length instructions more closely. A blinded human evaluation confirms that POLARIS-9B is preferred to the base Qwen3.5-9B and on par with Qwen3.5-27B. Despite training only on stories up to 4k words, POLARIS-9B preserves quality on prompts requesting stories up to 3 times the training length, a regime where most open-weight models degrade substantially in quality, length adherence, or both. More broadly, our results suggest that length generalization is a meaningful stress test for creative-writing models and a useful lens for distinguishing otherwise close models.

</details>


### [47] [Discourse-Role Labels as Presentation-Time Variables for Context Use in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04109)
*Jianguo Zhu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates how language model behavior changes based on the role-specific labels like 'Instruction' or 'Example' used to present misleading information, finding significant variations in adoption rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the influence of different wrapper labels on language model behavior, particularly when models handle potentially misleading context, and how these labels affect reliance on provided content.

Method: The study uses a paired fixed-content probe over 500 MMLU-Pro items where misleading assertions are wrapped with various discourse-role labels. Misleading Adoption Rates are analyzed across models like GPT-5.5 and Llama-3-8B-Instruct, alongside testing methods like paired tests and manual audits.

Result: Labels such as 'Instruction' and 'Reference' lead to high adoption of misleading content, while 'Example' reduces it significantly. Other factors like arithmetic tasks, nested-label conflicts, and external contexts also influence adoption rates.

Conclusion: Wrapper labels significantly impact the reliance of language models on supplied context, suggesting the need to regulate and report label usage in benchmarks and applications like RAG systems.

Abstract: Context-augmented language model systems often wrap supplied content with labels such as Reference:, Evidence:, Instruction:, Note:, or Example:, but the effect of these labels on reader-model behavior remains underexplored. We introduce a paired fixed-content probe over 500 MMLU-Pro items: each item receives the same misleading answer-bearing assertion under different discourse-role labels, and adoption is measured by whether the model outputs the injected wrong option. Across GPT-5.5, DeepSeek V4 Pro, Llama-3-8B-Instruct, and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, Misleading Adoption Rate shifts by 56-84 percentage points. Binding or source-like labels such as Instruction: and Reference: produce high adoption, whereas Example: consistently suppresses it. Paired tests, bootstrap intervals, final-instruction ablations, and Qwen final-step log-probability probes support a label-conditioned candidate preference. Boundary probes show where the effect weakens or persists: arithmetic tasks reduce adoption, passage-shaped external context preserves smaller label gaps, short-answer evaluation rules out option-letter copying, and nested-label conflicts suggest that illustrative framing can delimit adoption scope. A 200-case single-author manual audit confirms that the short-answer contrasts are stable under conservative adjudication. The resulting claim is bounded but practical: context-utilization and reader-side RAG benchmarks should report and control wrapper labels, because presentation choices can change measured reliance on supplied context.

</details>


### [48] [Computational conceptual history of scientific concepts: From early digital methods to LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04118)
*Michael Zichert,Arno Simons*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper discusses the role of large language models (LLMs) in concept analysis within the domains of history, philosophy, and sociology of science (HPSS), contrasting them with earlier computational approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap in understanding how LLMs can contribute uniquely to computational conceptual history while addressing challenges they inherit from earlier methods.

Method: The paper reviews and contrasts historical computational methods with LLM-based approaches in HPSS, examining challenges like corpus construction, operationalization, model selection, and evaluation.

Result: LLMs provide advancements in areas like lexical semantic change detection but also inherit and reformulate traditional methodological issues in new computational workflows.

Conclusion: LLMs offer novel contributions to HPSS but require careful consideration of traditional challenges, making their integration into computational conceptual history both promising and complex.

Abstract: This article situates large language models (LLMs) within the longer history of computational approaches to concept analysis in the history, philosophy, and sociology of science (HPSS). We examine what LLMs add to existing methods, how they inherit longstanding problems, and review recent case studies that employ them. In the first part, we reconstruct computational conceptual history before LLMs by bringing together three strands of work: early digital methods in HPSS, distributional approaches from digital history and related research, and lexical semantic change detection. We provide an overview of the main challenges and opportunities, focusing on corpus construction, operationalization and modelling choices, and evaluation and interpretation. In the second part, we turn to the era of LLMs, starting with a short introduction to LLMs before reviewing LLM-based work on lexical semantic change detection and relevant case studies in HPSS. We then revisit the earlier methodological questions, showing how issues of corpus construction, model choice and training data, operationalization trade-offs, and evaluation and interpretation play out in LLM-based workflows.

</details>


### [49] [SaliMory: Orchestrating Cognitive Memory for Conversational Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04120)
*Kai Zhang,Xinyuan Zhang,Hongda Jiang,Shiun-Zu Kuo,Hyokun Yun,Ejaz Ahmed,Shereen Oraby,Ziyun Li,Sanat Sharma,Ann Lee,Ahmed A Aly,Anuj Kumar,Raffay Hamid,Xin Luna Dong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces SALIMORY, a framework for training conversational agents with persistent memory, showing superior performance and reduced memory-related failures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conversational agents need persistent memory across interactions for longevity, but existing methods have issues like degraded reasoning and credit assignment bottlenecks.

Method: The SALIMORY framework utilizes a cognitively-structured memory and employs hierarchical stage-wise rewards and reward-decomposed contrastive refinement for end-to-end training.

Result: SALIMORY reduces memory failures by one-third, improves accuracy by over 10% against state-of-the-art methods, and doubles the Good Personalization rate.

Conclusion: The SALIMORY framework enhances conversational agents by better handling memory operations and providing significant performance improvements.

Abstract: Conversational agents that serve as lifelong companions must maintain persistent memory across all interactions. However, simply expanding context windows with raw retrieval degrades reasoning quality, while training memory agents via standard reinforcement learning creates a severe credit assignment bottleneck in a multi-stage pipeline. To solve this, we introduce SALIMORY, a framework that trains a single language model to manage a cognitively-structured memory-spanning user facts, preferences, and working memory. By introducing a hierarchical stage-wise process reward and reward-decomposed contrastive refinement, SALIMORY provides isolated supervision for distinct memory operations (selective filtering, consolidation, and cue-driven recall) end-to-end. SALIMORY cuts memory-attributed failures by one-third, outperforms the state-of-the-art by over 10% in end-to-end accuracy, and more than doubles the Good Personalization rate.

</details>


### [50] [When Retrieval Doesn't Help: A Large-Scale Study of Biomedical RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04127)
*Erfan Nourbakhsh,Rocky Slavin,Ke Yang,Anthony Rios*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper analyzes retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for medical question answering (QA) and finds minimal gains from retrieval methods compared to model choice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate whether retrieval-augmented generation methods consistently improve medical QA, given the high stakes of factual accuracy in this domain.

Method: The study examines five models (7B to 72B parameters), across ten biomedical QA datasets, four retrieval methods, and four retrieval corpora, comparing performance with and without retrieval.

Result: Retrieval methods lead to small, inconsistent improvements (1-2 points) over baselines without retrieval. Backbone models influence performance more than retrieval methods or sources.

Conclusion: The bottleneck in medical QA lies in models' limitations in leveraging retrieved evidence effectively, rather than retrieval quality itself.

Abstract: Medical question answering is a high-stakes setting where factual errors can have serious consequences. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is widely viewed as a promising solution, and prior work has reported substantial gains for large medical QA models. We revisit this assumption across a broad range of open-weight instruction-tuned models spanning 7B to 72B parameters. Across five models, ten biomedical QA datasets, four retrieval methods, and four retrieval corpora, we find that retrieval yields only small and inconsistent improvements over a no-retrieval baseline, typically within 1-2 points. In contrast, the choice of backbone model has a much larger effect than the choice of retriever or corpus, and expert and layman retrieval sources perform similarly in most settings. These results suggest that the main bottleneck is not retrieval quality alone, but the model's limited ability to use retrieved evidence effectively.

</details>


### [51] [Expert-Aware Refusal Steering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04160)
*Anna C. Marbut,Daniel R. Olson,Travis J. Wheeler*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper investigates how refusal behavior, or the ability of LLMs to decline harmful requests, can be manipulated in Mixture-of-Experts LLMs using steering methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and enhance safety alignment in instruction-tuned large language models by exploring refusal steering techniques specifically in Mixture-of-Experts architectures.

Method: The study applies a refusal steering method to three open-source Mixture-of-Experts LLMs, introduces two new expert-aware refusal steering methods, and analyzes their efficacy in steering refusal behavior by considering expert-specific routing and steering directions.

Result: The proposed steering methods effectively influence refusal behavior, even when relying on a single expert's output, and reveal that steering signals influencing refusal differ from expert routing patterns.

Conclusion: Refusal behavior in MoE LLMs can be steered effectively, providing safety alignment insights, and attention mechanisms play a significant role alongside expert routing patterns in shaping refusal responses.

Abstract: Safety alignment in instruction-tuned large language models (LLMs) depends on a model's ability to reliably refuse to respond to harmful or disallowed requests. Recent work has shown that a steering vector can be applied to a dense LLM during inference to effectively suppress refusal behavior, inducing response to harmful requests. We extend this refusal steering method to three open-source Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLMs and find that steering performance is uninhibited by the complex routing patterns inherent to the MoE architecture. We then propose two expert-aware refusal steering methods that leverage refusal-specific expert routing patterns and expert-specific steering directions to suppress normal refusal behavior. We find that refusal behavior can be effectively steered based on the output of a single expert. Our results show that refusal signals captured by steering methods differ from expert routing behavior, suggesting a substantial role for attention in MoE refusal behavior.

</details>


### [52] [A Systematic Analysis of Linguistic Features in AI-Generated Text Detection Across Domains and Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04177)
*Yassir El Attar,Esra Dönmez,Maximilian Maurer,Agnieszka Falenska*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The study assesses linguistic features to explain why text appears machine-generated and identifies robust indicators like lexical richness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the fragmented understanding of linguistic features that indicate AI-generated text across various models and domains.

Method: The authors conducted a large-scale empirical evaluation testing the effectiveness of 284 linguistic features on outputs from 27 LLMs and ten text domains.

Result: They found classifiers could reliably differentiate AI and human-written text using linguistic features, but most indicators were context-dependent except for lexical richness.

Conclusion: Lexical richness is a robust linguistic feature for identifying AI-generated text, offering a foundation for interpretable analyses across contexts.

Abstract: Interpretable linguistic features offer a promising approach for explaining why a given text appears machine-generated, particularly for non-expert users. However, existing findings on which features reliably indicate LLM-generated text remain fragmented across feature sets, models, and text domains. To address this gap, we conduct a large-scale empirical study assessing the robustness of linguistic signals for characterizing AI-generated text. Our analysis covers 284 interpretable linguistic features across outputs from 27 LLMs and ten text domains under cross-model and cross-domain generalization settings. We show that classifiers based solely on linguistic features can reliably distinguish AI-generated from human-written text. However, many previously proposed indicators prove strongly context-dependent, with the exception of measures of lexical richness, which remain robust signals across model families and text domains. These results demonstrate which linguistic signals generalize across contexts and provide a foundation for more reliable, interpretable analyses of AI-generated language.

</details>


### [53] [ACAT: A Collaborative Platform for Efficient Aspect-Based Sentiment Dataset Annotation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04189)
*Ana-Maria Luisa Mocanu,Ciprian-Octavian Truica,Elena-Simona Apostol*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces ACAT, a web-based tool for efficient, collaborative, and comprehensive annotation in Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA), featuring automatic ETL pipelines and high inter-annotator agreement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the inefficiencies in current annotation tools used for ABSA, which require manual processes for consolidating data, reconstructing relational structures, and computing reliability metrics.

Method: The authors developed ACAT, a web platform supporting four key ABSA workflows with features like collaborative annotation and automated ETL pipeline to streamline data preparation and calculation of inter-annotator agreement.

Result: ACAT was validated using 1,002 restaurant reviews annotated by two individuals. It achieved median annotation times of 31.58 seconds and high inter-annotator agreement with metrics ranging from 0.78 to 0.86.

Conclusion: ACAT significantly enhances the efficiency, reliability, and usability of ABSA annotation workflows, making it a valuable tool for generating high-quality training datasets.

Abstract: Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA) requires high-quality datasets to train reliable models. However, existing annotation tools treat output as flat files, leaving researchers to manually consolidate multi-annotator data, reconstruct relational structures, and compute reliability metrics through custom scripts. This paper introduces ACAT (Aspect-based sentiment analysis Collaborative Annotation Tool), a web-based platform natively supporting four ABSA workflows: (1) Aspect-Category Sentiment Analysis, (2) Clause-Level Segmentation, (3) Aspect-Term Sentiment Analysis with character-level position tracking, and (4) Aspect Sentiment Triplet Extraction with dual span offset preservation. Its core contribution is an automated Extract, Transform, Load (ETL) pipeline that aligns collaborative annotations and computes Inter-Annotator Agreement (IAA) metrics directly at export, yielding training-ready datasets. In a preliminary validation on 1,002 restaurant reviews with two annotators of differing expertise, ACAT achieves a median annotation time of 31.58 seconds and a raw IAA ranging from 0.78 to 0.86 across all tasks.

</details>


### [54] [Cross-Prompt Generalization in Detecting AI-Generated Fake News Using Interpretable Linguistic Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04199)
*Aya Vera-Jimenez,Samuel Jaeger,Calvin Ibenye,Dhrubajyoti Ghosh*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This study evaluates whether fake news classifiers can generalize detection across different AI-generated text prompts. A novel cross-prompt framework achieves high performance using linguistic features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Growing concerns about AI-generated fake news demand understanding whether detection models can generalize across different prompting techniques.

Method: The study analyzed fake and real news datasets, extracted linguistic features, and trained a random forest classifier to test performance across different train-test prompt combinations.

Result: The classifier achieved consistently high AUC values (0.988–1.000) across all cross-prompt combinations, with AI-generated text showing stable linguistic patterns like higher lexical diversity and reduced readability.

Conclusion: Linguistic features provide a reliable basis for detecting AI-generated fake news under varying prompt conditions, showcasing strong generalization potential.

Abstract: The increasing use of large language models has raised concerns about the spread of AI-generated fake news, particularly under varying prompting strategies. Most existing detection models are trained and evaluated under a single generation setting, leaving their ability to generalize across unseen prompts unclear. In this study, we investigate cross-prompt generalization in fake news detection using three datasets of AI-generated articles produced under distinct prompts, combined with real news articles. We extract interpretable linguistic features capturing lexical diversity, readability, and emotion-based characteristics and evaluate a random forest classifier under a cross-prompt framework, where models trained on one prompt are tested on another. Across all six train-test combinations, performance remains consistently high, with AUC values ranging from 0.988 to 1.000. Analysis of feature distributions shows that AI-generated text exhibits increased lexical diversity, reduced readability, and substantially lower emotional intensity compared to the overall dataset, with variations across prompts. Despite these distributional shifts, the classifier maintains strong performance, indicating that these features capture stable properties of AI-generated text that generalize across prompting strategies. These findings suggest that feature-based approaches can provide robust detection of AI-generated fake news under prompt variability.

</details>


### [55] [MM-BizRAG: Rethinking Multimodal Retrieval-Augmented Generation for General Purpose Enterprise Q&A](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04231)
*Hanoz Bhathena,Parin Rajesh Jhaveri,Rohan Mittal,Prateek Singh,Aymen Kallala,Rachneet Kaur,Yiqiao Jin,Zhen Zeng,Adwait Ratnaparkhi,Denis Kochedykov*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces MM-BizRAG, a multimodal retrieval-augmented generation system that explicitly incorporates document structure for handling enterprise documents, outperforming existing baselines significantly on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent MM-RAG approaches prioritize efficiency by minimal parsing, but often fail to handle structured information in enterprise documents adequately, relying heavily on implicit representations from pre-trained models.

Method: MM-BizRAG uses document structure-aware parsing with orientation-specific ingestion pipelines and placeholder-based alignment to handle vertically (e.g., reports) and horizontally structured (e.g., slide decks) documents. A unified LLM-driven pipeline maintains reading order and multimodal assembly decouples retrieval from generation.

Result: Experimental results show MM-BizRAG outperformed state-of-the-art baselines by up to 32% on a diverse enterprise dataset and benchmarks like SlideVQA and FinRAGBench-V, particularly excelling in report-style layouts.

Conclusion: By explicitly leveraging document structure, MM-BizRAG demonstrates its advantage over vision-centric baselines, while the proposed FastRAGEval metric further enhances evaluation of generative performance.

Abstract: Recent advances in multimodal retrieval-augmented generation (MM-RAG) have shifted toward minimal parsing, relying on page-level images for producing retriever embeddings and for answer generation. While efficient, this trend often neglects explicit handling of the rich, structured information in complex enterprise documents, instead depending on pre-trained embeddings or vision-language models to implicitly capture such structure. In this work, we take a more direct approach: MM-BizRAG proactively extracts and represents document structure via a document structure-aware split that dynamically routes documents through orientation-specific ingestion pipelines, applying explicit layout-aware parsing for vertically structured documents (e.g., reports) and holistic page-level representations for horizontally structured documents (e.g., slide decks). A unified LLM-driven artifact transformation pipeline with placeholder-based positional alignment preserves natural reading order, while inference-time multimodal assembly decouples retrieval representations from generation context, enabling richer, more grounded answers without any finetuning requirement. Through experiments on a large, heterogeneous enterprise dataset and two public benchmarks (SlideVQA and FinRAGBench-V), MM-BizRAG consistently outperforms state-of-the-art vision-centric baselines by up to 32% points, with especially strong gains on report-style layouts. Furthermore, we introduce FastRAGEval, a single-call LLM Judge metric for fine-grained generative recall that halves RAGChecker's cost while achieving stronger human alignment.

</details>


### [56] [Supportive Token Revealing for Fast Diffusion Language Model Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04236)
*Giries Abu Ayoub,Mario Barbara,Lluís Pastor-Pérez,Tanja Bien,Aneesh Barthakur,Alaa Maalouf,Loay Mualem*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces AXON, a training-free module for diffusion language models, designed to improve the quality-latency trade-off in parallel text generation by selecting contextually supportive tokens for decoding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the quality-latency trade-off in diffusion language models caused by either aggressive or overly conservative decoding strategies.

Method: The proposed mechanism, AXON, monitors masked tokens during decoding and selects anchors—confident masked tokens that uncertain positions depend on—using attention, uncertainty, and confidence signals.

Result: Experiments show AXON improves the efficiency and accuracy of parallel decoders in diffusion language models on reasoning and code-generation tasks, reducing decoding steps while maintaining or improving quality.

Conclusion: AXON successfully enhances existing parallel decoding strategies by focusing on confident token reveals that assist later denoising, improving decoding performance without retraining the model.

Abstract: Discrete diffusion language models can generate text efficiently by updating multiple masked positions in parallel, but this parallelism introduces a quality-latency trade-off. Aggressive decoding may commit mutually dependent tokens too early, while conservative decoding requires many denoising steps. Existing methods address this tension by deciding which tokens are safe to reveal using confidence or dependency criteria. However, avoiding unsafe commits does not necessarily make the remaining masked sequence easy to decode, since uncertain tokens may depend on masked tokens, creating a bottleneck for denoising steps. We propose AXON, a training-free module that can be added on top of existing parallel decoding strategies for diffusion language models. Rather than replacing the base decoder, AXON monitors the remaining uncertain masked tokens and intervenes only when their current state suggests that additional context is needed. It then shifts the criterion from which tokens are safest to reveal to which confident reveals would best support later denoising. AXON selects anchors, confident masked tokens that uncertain positions attend to, using attention, uncertainty, and confidence signals. Experiments on reasoning and code-generation benchmarks across multiple diffusion language models show that AXON improves the quality-latency trade-off of existing parallel decoders, often reducing the number of function evaluations while maintaining or improving accuracy.

</details>


### [57] [Can I Take Another Dose? Evaluating LLM Decision-Making Under Temporal Uncertainty in OTC Dosing QA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04262)
*Maroof Kousar,Yibo Hu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces DOSEBENCH, a benchmark for evaluating the ability of large language models (LLMs) to correctly answer over-the-counter (OTC) medication dosing questions, and identifies challenges like reasoning over rolling dose windows and handling ambiguity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address existing gaps in medical question-answering (QA) systems, especially for safety-critical OTC medication inquiries where understanding dose timing, intake limits, and label constraints are critical.

Method: The authors developed a benchmark dataset called DOSEBENCH, consisting of 81 curated OTC medication scenarios, focused on adult acetaminophen and ibuprofen use. They evaluated four LLMs on metrics like decision correctness, explanation verifiability, and confidence while analyzing 1,620 generated responses.

Result: The evaluated LLMs frequently faced challenges in handling rolling-window reasoning and ambiguity, with confident responses often still violating dosage constraints.

Conclusion: OTC dosing QA serves as an effective and practical testbed to evaluate LLMs on temporal reasoning, constraint adherence, and uncertainty handling, while highlighting existing limitations in their safety-related decision-making capabilities.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for everyday health questions, including whether a user can safely take another dose of an over-the-counter (OTC) medication. Yet this common safety-relevant setting remains underexplored in existing medical QA evaluations, where correct answers require tracking dose timing, computing rolling 24-hour intake, following product-label constraints, and handling incomplete medication histories. We introduce DOSEBENCH, a focused benchmark of 81 curated OTC dosing scenarios focused on adult acetaminophen and ibuprofen use, with manually annotated gold references. We evaluate four LLMs across repeated runs using metrics for decision correctness, consistency, explanation verifiability, failure types, and confidence-related signals, resulting in 1,620 model responses. Our results show that models frequently struggle with rolling-window reasoning and ambiguity-sensitive cases and that stable or confident-looking responses can still violate dosing constraints. These findings suggest that OTC dosing QA provides a narrow yet practical testbed for evaluating temporal reasoning, constraint following, and safety-relevant uncertainty handling in medical QA.

</details>


### [58] [Long Live Fine-Tuning: Task-Specific Transformers Outperform Zero-Shot LLMs for Misinformation Response Classification on Reddit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04274)
*JooYoung Lee,Lin Tian,Angela Brillantes,Adriana-Simona Mihăiţă,Marian-Andrei Rizoiu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper assesses the assumption that large-scale language models (LLMs) are sufficient for nuanced misinformation classification, finding that task-specific fine-tuned models like RoBERTa outperform zero-shot models (e.g., Llama, Claude) in detecting belief-class misinformation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate whether large-scale, general-purpose LLMs are effective for nuanced misinformation classification tasks without specific fine-tuning.

Method: The study tested nine models spanning paradigms such as pretrained general models (BART-MNLI, Llama), fine-tuned models (RoBERTa, DistilBERT), and commercial LLMs (Claude, Gemini) across 900 Reddit comments labeled into belief, fact-check, and other categories.

Result: Fine-tuned RoBERTa achieved the best performance with a 0.62 macro-F1 score, outperforming the best zero-shot model (Claude Haiku 4.5 at 0.50). The supervised advantage was most prominent in the belief class, which zero-shot models underperformed in.

Conclusion: Reliance on general-purpose LLMs for misinformation verification may be misleading. Task-specific fine-tuning is more effective, and model performance varies significantly based on label schema and topic.

Abstract: As large language models (LLMs) become default tools for online information verification, an implicit assumption follows them: that scale and general capability are sufficient for nuanced classification of misinformation discourse. We test this assumption directly on 900 Reddit comments spanning three PolitiFact-verified misinformation claims (environment, health, immigration), labelled as belief (propagates the claim), fact-check (corrects it), or other. We compare nine models across three paradigms -- BART-MNLI, three Llama variants, three commercial frontier LLMs (Claude Haiku 4.5, Gemini Flash Lite 2.5, Claude Sonnet 4.6), and fine-tuned DistilBERT and RoBERTa -- under universal and topic-specific label schemas.
  The assumption does not hold. Fine-tuned RoBERTa reaches 0.62 macro-$F_1$ against a best zero-shot result of 0.50 (Claude Haiku 4.5), at a fraction of the per-query cost; the supervised advantage is concentrated on the belief class, the implicit, affective category every zero-shot model under-detects. Scaling does not help: Llama-3-8B matches Llama-3-70B, and Claude Sonnet 4.6 underperforms the smaller Haiku under generic labels, collapsing belief detection to 0.17 and refusing outright on a subset of comments flagged as sensitive. This is a safety-alignment artefact, not a capacity limit. Label schema and topic jointly shape zero-shot performance, with the same model varying by more than 0.13 macro-$F_1$ across topics under matched labels. In a verification context, where missing belief is the costlier error, task-specific fine-tuning remains the more reliable choice despite the proliferation of large generative models.

</details>


### [59] [Using Text-Based Causal Inference to Disentangle Factors Influencing Online Review Ratings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04286)
*Linsen Li,Aron Culotta,Nicholas Mattei*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a method using CausalBERT with enhancements to analyze the impact of different aspects of online reviews on overall perception and validates this approach with U.S. school reviews.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Aspect-based sentiment analysis has lacked focus on understanding the individual impact of review aspects on overall perception, particularly due to correlations between these aspects.

Method: Employs an enhanced version of CausalBERT with improvements like temperature scaling, hyperparameter optimization, and interpretability methods to analyze textual review data as proxies for real-world attributes.

Result: Demonstrates that the proposed method delivers more reliable estimates and identifies school administration and benchmark performance as significant drivers of overall school ratings.

Conclusion: The methodology effectively disentangles aspect-level impacts on overall ratings, offering improved tools and insights for analyzing online review data.

Abstract: Online reviews provide valuable insights into the perceived quality of facets of a product or service. While aspect-based sentiment analysis has focused on extracting these facets from reviews, there is less work understanding the impact of each aspect on overall perception.
