Method: Created a benchmark involving multi-agent simulations modeling users as persistent worlds with layered profiles and dynamic trajectories, designed to evaluate memory tracking, user understanding, privacy control, and emotional companionship.

Result: Through tests on 2,000 personas and 111K tasks, results show that even state-of-the-art memory models struggle to maintain accurate user understanding and provide meaningful companionship over extended timeframes.

Conclusion: Existing models fail to sustain long-term user comprehension and companionship, underscoring the need for more robust benchmarks and algorithmic improvements in lifelong digital companions.

Abstract: Lifelong digital companions must integrate cross-session cues, continually update their understanding of users, and adapt to shifting privacy boundaries. Existing evaluations fail to capture this, testing memory recall and short-term empathy in isolation. To bridge this gap, we introduce \benchmark, a benchmark centered on multi-session \textit{Memory-Emotion-Environment} loops. By modeling users as persistent worlds with layered profiles and event trajectories, \benchmark uses multi-agent simulation to project environmental dynamics into dialogue, preserving the critical gap between latent thoughts and observable expressions. Evaluating 2,000 personas and 111K tasks across memory tracking, user understanding, privacy control, and emotional companionship, our experiment results reveal a stark reality: even models that saturate current memory benchmarks fail to sustain accurate user understanding and true companionship over long horizons.

</details>


### [91] [CRAFT: Cost-aware Refinement And Front-aware Tuning of Prompts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04661)
*Shanu Kumar,Shubhanshu Khandelwal,Akhila Yesantarao Venkata,Parag Agrawal,Yova Kementchedjhieva,Manish Gupta*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces CRAFT, a novel algorithm for optimizing prompts based on the trade-off between accuracy and cost, avoiding scalarization collapse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prompt optimization for large language models often struggles with balancing accuracy and cost, and traditional methods fail to explore an optimal trade-off between these two objectives.

Method: CRAFT combines complementary generators for accuracy and cost edits, Pareto-gap acquisition for resource budgeting, and NSGA-II retention to maintain diverse solutions.

Result: Through experiments on six benchmarks, CRAFT successfully identifies high-accuracy and low-cost solutions, outperforming traditional approaches that focus narrowly on either accuracy or cost.

Conclusion: CRAFT enables flexible post-search decisions by retaining diverse prompt solutions, effectively addressing the accuracy-cost trade-off that prior methods often fail to optimize.

Abstract: Prompts tuned for accuracy often grow long, raising inference cost on every model call. The best accuracy-cost trade-off depends on the task and the budget, so prompt optimization is a search over the Pareto front of accuracy and prompt-token cost rather than for one prompt. The usual shortcut, collapsing the objectives into a weighted sum, fixes the trade-off weight before search and often recovers only a narrow region of the front, a failure we call scalarization collapse. We present CRAFT (Cost-aware Refinement And Front-aware Tuning), a Pareto-front prompt optimizer that treats target-LLM validation calls as the scarce resource and allocates them to candidates near the optimistic candidate front. Each round, complementary accuracy-oriented and cost-oriented generators propose edits, Pareto-gap acquisition spends the per-round validation budget, and NSGA-II retention keeps a spread-out population. Across six classification and reasoning benchmarks, CRAFT's retained fronts reach both high-accuracy and low-cost regions, while accuracy-only, cost-only, and weighted-sum baselines each concentrate in narrower regions. The accuracy-cost trade-off becomes a post-search choice, not a pre-search weight.

</details>


### [92] [SMADE-IE: Sparse Multi-Agent Framework with Evidence-Driven Debate for Zero-Shot Information Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04691)
*Kenfeng Huang,Yi Cai,Xin Wu,Zikun Deng,Li Yuan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SMADE-IE is a new framework for zero-shot information extraction (IE) using large language models. It performs better than prior methods by reducing errors and being more efficient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the limitations of existing zero-shot IE methods, which suffer from errors in monolithic prompting and inefficiency in cross-type or multi-agent debate methods.

Method: The paper introduces SMADE-IE, which uses an Adaptive Mode Selector for input routing and an Evidence-Driven Debate mechanism for resolving conflicts. These methods improve extraction accuracy and efficiency.

Result: SMADE-IE outperforms other zero-shot IE approaches across 9 benchmark datasets for NER, RE, and JERE tasks, while also improving token usage efficiency.

Conclusion: The proposed SMADE-IE framework significantly enhances zero-shot IE by reducing errors, improving efficiency, and leveraging well-structured conflict resolution.

Abstract: Zero-shot information extraction (IE) with large language models (LLMs) has attracted increasing attention due to its flexibility in adapting to new schemas and domains without task-specific training. Existing approaches mainly rely on monolithic prompting, each-type prompting, or multi-agent debate. However, monolithic prompting often suffers from boundary and type errors, while each-type prompting and multi-agent debate introduce cross-type conflicts, redundant agent interactions, and substantial token overhead. To address these challenges, we propose SMADE-IE, a sparse and evidence-driven multi-agent framework for zero-shot IE. SMADE-IE first employs an Adaptive Mode Selector to dynamically route inputs into either a lightweight Global Extraction Mode or a Type-Centric Extraction Mode, reducing unnecessary type selection and reasoning noise. For conflicting predictions, we further introduce an Evidence-Driven Debate mechanism that structures arguments into Toulmin-style components and performs confidence aggregation through external evidence scoring and Bayesian updates. Experimental results on 9 benchmark datasets across NER, RE, and JERE tasks show that SMADE-IE consistently outperforms existing zero-shot IE baselines while also improving token efficiency through sparse agent selection and early-stopping debate.

</details>


### [93] [DuDi: Dual-Signal Distillation with Cross-Lingual Verbalizer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04694)
*Patomporn Payoungkhamdee,Tinnakit Udsa,Jian Gang Ngui,Sarana Nutanong,Alham Fikri Aji,Peerat Limkonchotiwat*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces DuDi, a multilingual distillation framework enhancing small language models' (SLMs) performance for Southeast Asian (SEA) languages, significantly outperforming existing baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SLMs are resource-efficient but underperform significantly in multilingual tasks, especially for underrepresented SEA languages, motivating the need for improved methods to maintain their performance across languages.

Method: DuDi, a dual-signal distillation framework, incorporates a combination of sequence-level and token-level signals using an online approach, alongside a cross-lingual verbalizer for refining teacher-student knowledge transfer.

Result: Experimental results on SEA-HELM confirm that DuDi outperforms existing distillation methods consistently across various model families, sizes, and teacher-student setups.

Conclusion: DuDi effectively addresses the challenges of multilingual distillation for SEA languages in SLMs, providing transferable and complementary learning signals that enhance performance across scales.

Abstract: Small language models (SLMs) are efficient and scalable, but their multilingual capabilities degrade severely at sub-billion scales, especially for Southeast Asian (SEA) languages. We introduce DuDi, a dual-signal multilingual distillation framework that combines an online sequence-level signal with off-policy and on-policy token-level signals. DuDi further uses a cross-lingual verbalizer to refine teacher feedback and improve teacher-student transferability in multilingual settings. Experiments on SEA-HELM across multiple model families, scales, and teacher-student settings show that DuDi consistently outperforms competitive distillation baselines. Ablations and analyses confirm that sequence-level optimization, token-level supervision, and cross-lingual verbalization provide complementary and transferable learning signals for multilingual SLMs.

</details>


### [94] [Rethinking Continual Experience Internalization for Self-Evolving LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04703)
*Jingwen Chen,Wenkai Yang,Shengda Fan,Wenbo Nie,Chenxing Sun,Shaodong Zheng,Yangen Hu,Lu Pan,Ke Zeng,Yankai Lin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a method to improve continual learning in large language models (LLMs) by addressing progressive capability collapse in multi-iteration experience learning through systematic analysis of experience internalization dimensions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivated by the need to enable continual learning in LLMs by converting past contextual experiences into reusable capabilities, overcoming limitations of single-iteration transfer approaches.

Method: The authors systematically analyze experience internalization across three dimensions: experience granularity (favoring principle-level experience over instance-level), injection patterns (step-wise over global), and internalization regimes (off-policy context-distillation over on-policy).

Result: The study uncovers key failures in existing methods and presents a robust method for stable experience internalization, improving long-term capabilities in LLMs.

Conclusion: The proposed method provides concrete guidance for designing self-evolving, continuously learning LLMs by stabilizing and sustaining multi-iteration experience learning.

Abstract: Experience internalization converts contextual experience from past interactions into reusable parametric capability, offering a promising path toward continual learning in large language models (LLMs). While prior work has predominantly focused on single-iteration transfer, we discover that under multi-iteration experience learning, existing methods suffer from a progressive capability collapse rather than compounding improvement. We systematically examine this failure through three vital dimensions of experience internalization: (1) Experience Granularity: We find that principle-level experience is more durable than instance-level experience, as it effectively abstracts transferable strategies away from trajectory-specific details. (2) Experience Injection Pattern: Our analysis reveals that step-wise injection significantly outperforms global injection by aligning experience with intermediate decision states, a property that is critical for long-horizon tool use. (3) Internalization Regime: We demonstrate that off-policy context-distillation on high-quality teacher trajectories provides a substantially more stable training signal than on-policy context-distillation, which is inherently limited by local corrections on student-induced flawed states. Together, these insights yield a simple yet robust recipe for stable and sustainable experience internalization, providing concrete guidance for engineering self-evolving and continually learning LLMs.

</details>


### [95] [Query-based Cross-Modal Projector Bolstering Mamba Multimodal LLM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04719)
*SooHwan Eom,Jay Shim,Gwanhyeong Koo,Haebin Na,Mark A. Hasegawa-Johnson,Sungwoong Kim,Chang D. Yoo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper proposes a query-based cross-modal projector for the Mamba framework to improve computational efficiency and performance in vision-language modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address and mitigate the quadratic computational complexity of Transformers in large language models, particularly for vision-language tasks.

Method: The paper introduces a cross-modal projector leveraging cross-attention to compress visual tokens and eliminate the need for manually designed 2D scan orders in Mamba's input sequence conversion for multimodal modeling.

Result: The experimental results demonstrate enhanced performance and throughput for Mamba-based large language models across various vision-language benchmarks.

Conclusion: The cross-modal projector successfully improves the efficiency and effectiveness of Mamba for vision-language understanding, showcasing its potential for wider applications in multimodal LLMs.

Abstract: The Transformer's quadratic complexity with input length imposes an unsustainable computational load on large language models (LLMs). In contrast, the Selective Scan Structured State-Space Model, or Mamba, addresses this computational challenge effectively. This paper explores a query-based cross-modal projector designed to bolster Mamba's efficiency for vision-language modeling by compressing visual tokens based on input through the cross-attention mechanism. This innovative projector also removes the need for manually designing the 2D scan order of original image features when converting them into an input sequence for Mamba LLM. Experimental results across various vision-language understanding benchmarks show that the proposed cross-modal projector enhances Mamba-based multimodal LLMs, boosting both performance and throughput.

</details>


### [96] [Multilingual Long-Form Speech Instruction Following: KIT's Submission to IWSLT 2026](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04730)
*Enes Yavuz Ugan,Maike Züfle,Yuka Ko,Supriti Sinhamahapatra,Fabian Retkowski,Seymanur Akti,Jan Niehues,Alexander Waibel*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents KIT's approach for the IWSLT Instruction Following Track using data augmentation and decoding techniques across multiple tasks and languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges posed by evolving instruction-based language models and the inclusion of new tasks, including an unknown surprise task that resists overfitting to known tasks.

Method: The approach involves augmenting training data via segment concatenation, label generation, and cross-lingual translation, supported by over 1M instances across tasks and languages. It also explores decoding techniques like likelihood-based reranking and Minimum Bayes Risk decoding.

Result: The study finds that likelihood-based reranking degrades semantic tasks due to issues with segmented audio processing, but this limitation is resolved with a hybrid decoding approach.

Conclusion: The proposed techniques effectively tackle diverse tasks in instruction-based systems, emphasizing the importance of decoding strategies and training data augmentation for improved performance.

Abstract: With the advent of Large Language Models, single-task and token-based multi-task models have evolved into instruction-based systems that infer task and target language implicitly from natural language prompts. This trend is reflected in IWSLT's Instruction Following Track, which this year introduced new tasks including an unknown surprise task, posing a genuine challenge against overfitting to known tasks. We present KIT's submission to the Long and Short Instruction Following tracks in the unconstrained setting. Our approach combines a general data augmentation pipeline that converts short-form corpora into long-form training data through segment concatenation, LLM-based label generation, and cross-lingual translation, yielding over 1M instances across six tasks and four languages. We further show that likelihood-based re-ranking, while highly effective for ASR, systematically degrades semantic tasks by spuriously selecting candidates generated from segmented audio processing rather than holistic long-form inference, a failure mode resolved by combining likelihood with Minimum Bayes Risk decoding.

</details>


### [97] [TIDE: Proactive Multi-Problem Discovery via Template-Guided Iteration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04743)
*Soyeong Jeong,Jinheon Baek,Minki Kang,Sung Ju Hwang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces TIDE, a framework for discovering multiple hidden problems in user contexts and addressing them iteratively with templates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitation of current agents that only act on explicit user requests, failing to identify hidden problems within the broader user context.

Method: The TIDE framework combines iterative discovery (surfacing problems in rounds and extending coverage) and thought templates (schemas guiding the grounding of problems in evidence).

Result: Experiments in personal workspaces and software repositories demonstrated that TIDE outperforms baselines in problem coverage, identification, and resolution.

Conclusion: TIDE effectively discovers and addresses hidden contextual problems by iteratively uncovering evidence-based insights and systematically resolving them through templates.

Abstract: Agents are widely deployed as assistants over documents, tools, and code. However, they typically act only on explicit user requests, which surface only the problems the user has noticed, while many other important problems coexist, hidden in plain sight, within the broader user context, with their total number unknown in advance. We frame this as the task of discovering multiple hidden problems from context, in which coexisting problems should be uncovered, grounded in supporting evidence, and paired with concrete actions. To this end, we introduce TIDE, a template-guided iterative framework with two complementary mechanisms. Specifically, motivated by the observation that single-pass prediction anchors on the most salient cases and yields generic claims, we propose iterative discovery, which surfaces a small batch of candidates per round while conditioning on what has already been found, so subsequent rounds extend coverage; and thought templates, reusable schemas distilled from previously solved cases that specify what contextual signals to attend to and how to connect them, anchoring each prediction in a recognizable problem class. We validate TIDE on two realistic settings, personal workspaces and software repositories, across four model backbones, showing substantial gains over single-shot and parallel multi-agent baselines on task coverage, identification, and resolution.

</details>


### [98] [PersonaTree: Structured Lifecycle Memory for Person Understanding in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04780)
*Yubo Hou,Jingwei Song,Hongbo Zhang,Zhisheng Chen,Bang Xiao,Tao Wan,Zengchang Qin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces 'PersonaTree,' a memory framework for persistent LLM agents to abstract interaction evidence into person-level understanding and claims. It achieves significant improvements across various benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations in existing memory methods for LLM agents that focus on retention and retrieval but fail to explicitly form person-level understanding through interaction evidence.

Method: PersonaTree organizes memory into a structured three-level persona tree, focusing on conservative writing, confidence-based consolidation, and query-conditioned path retrieval to enable better interaction evidence abstraction.

Result: PersonaTree achieved top rankings in 12 out of 18 compact scores across six benchmarks and demonstrated improved person understanding and alignment under strict context budgets.

Conclusion: The structured and hierarchical approach of PersonaTree improves the ability of LLM agents to understand and abstract person-level insights, outperforming traditional methods in memory-based tasks.

Abstract: Persistent LLM agents require memory representations that make the formation of person understanding explicit across long term interaction. Existing agent memory methods emphasize information retention and retrieval, yet give limited account of how accumulated interaction evidence is abstracted into person understanding. We view this process as schema formation, where situated evidence is abstracted into reusable patterns and stable person level claims. We introduce PersonaTree, a structured lifecycle memory framework that realizes this view as a three level persona tree with explicit support paths from evidence to claims. PersonaTree maintains the tree through conservative writing, confidence guided consolidation, and query conditioned path retrieval, returning only the evidence depth required by each query. Across six person understanding and persistent memory benchmarks with three answer backbones, PersonaTree ranks first in 12 of 18 compact scores and reaches the top two in 16 settings. Ablations show that hierarchy improves abstract person understanding on KnowMe, while support path retrieval improves RealPref alignment under a comparable context budget.

</details>


### [99] [A French Corpus Annotated for Multiword Expressions with Adverbial Function](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04828)
*Eric Laporte,Takuya Nakamura,Stavroula Voyatzi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This study provides a French corpus annotated for adverbial MWEs, useful for linguistic investigations in information retrieval and syntactic parsing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate MWEs with adverbial function for enhancement in computational linguistics tasks like parsing and extraction.

Method: Annotation of MWEs within a corpus, detailing types of annotated MWEs, resources, and methodologies applied.

Result: Developed an annotated French corpus specifically targeting adverbial MWEs, accessible under the LGPLLR license.

Conclusion: The corpus serves as a resource to advance research in linguistic parsing and information extraction for French language tools.

Abstract: This paper presents a French corpus annotated for multiword expressions (MWEs) with adverbial function. This corpus is designed for investigation on information retrieval and extraction, as well as on deep and shallow syntactic parsing. We delimit which kind of MWEs we annotated, we describe the resources and methods we used for the annotation, and we briefly comment the results. The annotated corpus is available at http://infolingu.univ-mlv.fr/ under the LGPLLR license.

</details>


### [100] [Large Language Models in K-12 Education: Alignment with State Curriculum Standards and Student Personas](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04846)
*Lisa Korver,Tomo Lazovich,Sherief Reda*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates how well large language models (LLMs) align with U.S. state-specific curriculum standards, particularly in the context of U.S. History teaching.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the potential ethical and educational implications of using LLMs in classrooms, focusing on whether these models align with varying U.S. state curriculum standards.

Method: An LLM-based pipeline was developed to compare U.S. History curricula across states. Controlled experiments were also conducted to test LLM responses to variations in user personas, including geographic location, grade level, gender, and race.

Result: The study found that while LLMs can adjust their portrayal of historical topics, these adjustments may reflect perceived political biases rather than actual curriculum standards. Models adapt well to different grade levels but show minimal sensitivity to race or gender.

Conclusion: The findings underscore the risks of misaligned LLMs impacting student learning due to deviations from state-specific curriculum standards and point to the need for better alignment methodologies.

Abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) become increasingly popular in educational settings, they raise important questions about the ethical implications of their use. Publicly available online chatbots are quickly improving in capability and accuracy leading to more widespread use, including among students looking for help with their homework. This makes it crucial to consider whether these models are aligned with educational standards. Because curriculum standards in the United States are set at the state level, they differ significantly in required content, emphasis, and narrative focus. In this work, we develop an LLM-based pipeline to identify variations in U.S. History curricula across states and evaluate the extent to which different LLMs reflect these state-specific curricular differences. In addition, we conduct controlled experiments that vary user personas by stating user attributes such as geographic location, grade level, gender and race to evaluate the sensitivity of LLM responses to user characteristics. We find that while models are able to adjust their presentation of historical topics, these shifts may come from the perceived political leanings of states and do not necessarily reflect actual curriculum content. Additionally, models successfully adapt to a student's grade level while showing minimal sensitivity to race or gender, suggesting they are capable of useful adaptation to student personas with limited demographic bias. Together, these findings highlight potential risks that open access to LLM chatbots may cause to student learning outcomes stemming from misalignment with state curriculum standards and highlight the need for more robust alignment techniques.

</details>


### [101] [Agent Planning Benchmark: A Diagnostic Framework for Planning Capabilities in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04874)
*Haoyu Sun,Wenxuan Wang,Mingyang Song,Jujie He,Weinan Zhang,Yang Liu,Yang Yang,Yu Cheng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces the Agent Planning Benchmark (APB) to evaluate LLM agents' planning capabilities separately from their execution skills. APB includes 4,209 multimodal cases across multiple settings and domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks often fail to isolate planning from execution in LLM agent evaluations, making it difficult to analyze specific planning shortcomings.

Method: The authors designed APB, a diagnostic benchmark with diverse multimodal cases to evaluate aspects of planning like tool robustness and feasibility reasoning, and validated it on other task datasets like ToolSandbox.

Result: APB uncovered systemic weaknesses in LLM agents' planning skills, such as robustness to noise and refinement during inference. It also improved plan accuracy and downstream execution effects when implemented.

Conclusion: APB serves as an important diagnostic tool for assessing and improving planning in LLM agents, complementing existing execution-focused benchmarks.

Abstract: Planning is central to LLM agents: before acting, an agent must decompose goals, select tools, reason over constraints, and decide when a task is infeasible. Yet existing agent evaluations often report only end-to-end success, making it difficult to determine whether failures stem from planning or execution. We introduce \textbf{Agent Planning Benchmark (APB)}, a planning-specific diagnostic benchmark with 4,209 multimodal cases across 22 domains and five settings, covering holistic planning, feedback-conditioned step-wise planning, and robustness under extraneous tools, broken tools, and unsolvable tasks. Across 12 MLLMs, APB reveals systematic weaknesses in long-horizon planning, tool-noise robustness, calibrated refusal, and inference-time refinement. We further validate APB on 200 ToolSandbox tasks and 200 $τ^2$-bench tasks, where APB-guided refinement consistently improves plan correctness, plan grade, and downstream execution metrics across three representative models. APB thus serves as an upstream diagnostic complement to execution benchmarks.

</details>


### [102] [Optimizing the Cost-Quality Tradeoff of Agentic Theorem Provers in Lean](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04883)
*Kári Rögnvaldsson,Chenhao Sun,Jasper Dekoninck,Martin Vechev*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces an action routing agent for Lean-based theorem proving workflows, reducing computational cost while maintaining performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the prohibitive computational costs in generating formal proofs using large language models (LLMs) in Lean, especially considering many failed proof attempts.

Method: The paper proposes a two-plane architecture: a data plane for lemma decomposition, formalization, and sampling, and a control plane for learning from failed attempts to evaluate success likelihood and cost to decide next steps.

Result: On PutnamBench subset, the agent achieved a $25.8\%$ reduction in computational cost on average compared to a fixed-step baseline without diminishing performance.

Conclusion: The use of failed proof trajectories as a signal for resource allocation demonstrates potential for more cost-efficient agentic theorem proving workflows.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used in workflows for generating formal proofs in Lean. These workflows often decompose problems into smaller lemmas, sample many proof attempts, and use compiler feedback to guide search. However, they can be prohibitively expensive, often spending substantial compute on attempts that ultimately fail. In this work, we address this problem with an action routing agent that consists of a data plane and a control plane. The data plane generates natural-language lemma decompositions, formalizes them in Lean, and samples proof attempts for the resulting theorem and lemma targets. The control plane observes previous failed Lean attempts, estimates both the likelihood of success and cost of another attempt, and decides whether to continue proving the current target or restart from a new breakdown. On a subset of PutnamBench, our agent decreases the cost by $25.8\%$ over a fixed-step baseline on average, preserving performance while using substantially less compute. These results suggest that failed Lean trajectories provide actionable signals for cost-aware resource allocation in agentic theorem proving.

</details>


### [103] [GRAIL: Gradient-Reweighted Advantages for Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04889)
*Tej Deep Pala,Vernon Toh,Soujanya Poria*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces GRAIL, a method that improves reinforcement learning in LLMs by reweighting token-wise advantages using gradient-activation saliency, outperforming existing methods like GRPO.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing reinforcement learning approaches for LLMs assign uniform sequence-level advantages or use costly process reward models, diluting gradient signals and misweighting token contribution. There's a need for a method that ensures fine-grained reasoning alignment without high computational costs.

Method: The authors propose GRAIL, an intrinsic method that uses gradient-activation saliency to reweight token-wise contributions to the reward. Tokens more sensitive to the final answer are weighted more heavily, providing a more accurate gradient signal.

Result: GRAIL was tested on five models from the Qwen3, R1-distilled, and OctoThinker families. It achieved a 3.60% improvement in accuracy and a 3.05% improvement in Pass@3 compared to GRPO.

Conclusion: GRAIL effectively aligns reasoning in LLMs without the need for costly process reward models, offering a significant improvement over existing sequence-level methods like GRPO.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (e.g. GRPO) is now a common way to improve mathematical reasoning in Large Language Models (LLMs). However, current methods usually broadcast one sequence-level advantage to all tokens, or use costly process reward models (PRMs) for step-level supervision. Uniform advantage distribution assumes that all tokens contribute equally to the final reward. This dilutes the gradient signal, since flawed reasoning steps and filler words are updated as strongly as valid logical inferences. To address this, we introduce Gradient-Reweighted Advantage (GRAIL), an intrinsic token-wise advantage reweighting method. GRAIL uses gradient-activation saliency to place more weight on tokens that are more locally sensitive to the final answer. Evaluations across five models from the Qwen3, R1-distilled and OctoThinker families show that GRAIL consistently outperforms GRPO. GRAIL achieved an average improvement of 3.60% in accuracy and 3.05% in Pass@3, demonstrating that fine-grained reasoning alignment can be achieved without process-level supervision.

</details>


### [104] ['Your AI Text is not Mine': Redefining and Evaluating AI-generated Text Detection under Realistic Assumptions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04906)
*Nils Dycke,Marina Sakharova,Nico Daheim,Iryna Gurevych*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper focuses on defining harmful uses of AI-generated text and introduces AITDNA, a benchmark dataset with detailed genesis data. It finds that existing detectors are inadequate for broad detection purposes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fill the gap in understanding what constitutes harmful or problematic uses of AI-generated text, as criteria in current approaches often lack consistency with real-world needs.

Method: Systematically define various notions of AI-generated text, collect a new dataset (AITDNA) with detailed editing and interaction histories, and benchmark existing detection methods.

Result: Existing machine-generated text detectors perform well for specific applications but fail as general-purpose tools. The authors provide a public dataset and code.

Conclusion: The work highlights the limitations of current detection systems and emphasizes the need for generalizable solutions. AITDNA dataset offers a resource for future research.

Abstract: Although it is generally agreed that AI-generated text poses a broad societal risk, there is no common understanding in the AI-generated text detection literature on what constitutes harmful use. Rather, existing datasets and approaches often define their own criteria and make their own assumptions, sometimes implicitly, and often only loosely related to real-world needs and applications. To address this gap, we here systematically define various notions of AI-generated text and their characteristics. To study these, we collect AITDNA - a new benchmark of human-machine co-constructed texts that is annotated with detailed genesis information, such as the entire edit and AI-interaction history. We benchmark various machine-generated text detectors and find that they often only perform well for specific notions but not as broad detectors. We release code and data publicly.

</details>


### [105] [Caliper: Probing Lexical Anchors versus Causal Structure in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04915)
*Zhenyu Yu,Shuigeng Zhou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper evaluates whether language model performance on causal reasoning tasks stems from true structural reasoning or superficial lexical pattern matching by anonymizing semantic variables.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether LLMs genuinely engage in structural causal reasoning or rely on lexical cues when performing causal reasoning tasks.

Method: The authors introduce 'Caliper,' a method involving controlled perturbations that anonymize semantic variable names while keeping underlying causal structures intact. They then test LLMs of varying sizes (3.8B-671B parameters) on three benchmarks under this anonymization schema.

Result: Anonymization of variables led to significant performance drops across most LLMs, indicating heavy reliance on lexical patterns rather than structural reasoning. Interventions such as structured scaffolding reduced these gaps by affecting smaller models' baseline performance without recovering true causal reasoning capabilities.

Conclusion: Current instruction-tuned LLMs evaluated zero-shot exhibit minimal evidence of structural causal reasoning and heavily depend on lexical anchors for causal reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Large language models reach 50 to 70% accuracy on causal reasoning benchmarks such as CLadder, but it is unclear whether this reflects structural reasoning or lexical pattern matching. We introduce Caliper, a controlled perturbation that replaces semantic variable names with placeholder tokens while preserving the causal graph and probabilistic specification of each question. Across nine instruction-tuned LLMs from 3.8B to 671B and three causal reasoning benchmarks, lexical anonymization yields robust accuracy drops of +7.6, +27.0, and +11.1 pp on a local 3.8B-14B set, rising to +29.6 and +18.0 pp on CRASS and e-CARE across nine frontier models spanning the 2024-2026 generations. Of 40 engaged model-by-benchmark cells, 39 show a positive gap, and the gap collapses by 17x on CLadder's pseudoword subset. Structured scaffolding and few-shot in-context learning each narrow the gap, but mainly by lowering P0 accuracy on smaller models rather than recovering P1. Current instruction-tuned LLMs, evaluated zero-shot, show little evidence of structural causal reasoning once lexical anchors are removed.

</details>


### [106] [Can Crowdsourcing Survive the LLM Era? A Community Survey on Human Data Collection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04924)
*Aswathy Velutharambath,Neele Falk,Sofie Labat,Tarun Tater,Amelie Wuehrl*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates how researchers address Large Language Model (LLM) usage in crowdsourced free-text datasets, surveying 155 NLP researchers to identify challenges, strategies, and implications for data quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to understand the impact of LLM utilization by crowdworkers on the quality of crowdsourced free-text datasets and to improve data collection practices in the research community.

Method: The authors conducted a survey with 155 researchers in NLP and related fields to gather insights on their experiences and strategies for detecting LLM-generated data in crowdsourcing tasks.

Result: The study found that 44% of researchers observed LLM-generated content in their data, with most aware of its potential use but unsure of effective precautionary measures. Textual style and quick task completion were common detection strategies.

Conclusion: The research community is aware of the issue posed by LLM usage in crowdsourcing but current strategies are insufficient. The authors propose guidelines for future data collection to address these challenges more effectively.

Abstract: The widespread use of Large Language Models (LLMs) as writing tools challenges the validity of crowdsourced data, as crowdworkers may outsource tasks to models. To better understand how this is addressed, we surveyed 155 researchers in NLP and related disciplines about their experiences and opinions on collecting free-text responses via crowdsourcing. This paper provides an overview of practitioners' challenges, mitigation strategies, and the foreseen implications on data quality. 44% of respondents reported observing LLM usage in their crowdsourced data. While 93% of them had anticipated this, half were unsure what precautions to take. The most prevalent detection strategies are distinctive textual style patterns and unusually fast completion times. Overall, survey responses show that the research community is aware of the problem and taking measures, but existing efforts remain insufficient to fully address it. Finally, we derive a set of considerations to guide future crowdsourced free-text data collection in the era of LLMs.

</details>


### [107] [SemBlock: Semantic Boundary Dynamic Blocks for Diffusion LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04964)
*Xinrui Song,Zhuoran Wang,Mingju Gao,Hao Tang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces SemBlock, a dynamic block decoding framework for diffusion language models that aligns blocks with semantic boundaries, improving decoding performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing blockwise decoding methods for diffusion language models often use rigid or signal-based approaches that fail to align with natural semantic boundaries, impeding text generation quality.

Method: The authors present SemBlock, which employs lightweight predictors trained on frozen LLaDA hidden states to dynamically construct blocks based on semantically predicted boundaries. They create the SemBound dataset for supervision, containing boundary labels derived from discourse structures across various tasks.

Result: SemBlock demonstrated consistent performance improvements compared to fixed-block decoding and AdaBlock across several tasks, including GSM8K, IFEval, MATH, and HumanEval.

Conclusion: SemBlock offers a practical and effective solution to enhance block decoding in diffusion LLMs, aligning it with semantic boundaries and outperforming existing methods. The framework is publicly available for further research.

Abstract: Diffusion language models (DLMs) generate text through iterative denoising, and blockwise decoding improves their practicality by committing tokens in local blocks. However, existing blockwise methods typically rely on fixed block sizes or delimiter-based runtime signals, which do not necessarily align with semantic boundaries. In this paper, we propose SemBlock, a semantic-boundary-driven dynamic block decoding framework for diffusion LLMs. SemBlock formulates dynamic block construction as semantic boundary prediction and trains lightweight predictors on frozen LLaDA hidden states. To provide supervision, we construct SemBound, a semantic-boundary dataset that derives boundary labels from discourse units, reasoning steps, and implementation spans across natural language, math, and code tasks. During inference, SemBlock uses predicted boundary probabilities to select the ending position of each dynamic block. Experiments on GSM8K, IFEval, MATH, and HumanEval show that SemBlock consistently improves over fixed-block decoding and AdaBlock. Our code is publicly available: https://github.com/TH-AI-Lab-PKU/SemBlock.

</details>


### [108] [SAID: Accelerating Diffusion-Based Language Models via Scaffold-Aware Iterative Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04974)
*Na Li,Chengda Wang,Mingju Gao,Hao Tang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces SAID, a framework to accelerate diffusion large language models (DLLMs) by redistributing computation among tokens based on their role and confidence level, achieving up to 9.1x faster inference while maintaining performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the high computational cost associated with inference in diffusion large language models (DLLMs), which, despite parallel updates, require many denoising steps for high-quality output.

Method: The paper proposes the Scaffold-Aware Iterative Decoding (SAID) framework, which focuses denoising computations on scaffold tokens to build semantic structure first and reduces computation for predictable detail tokens. It also introduces Confidence-Hierarchical Layered Generation (CHLG) to allocate more steps to low-confidence tokens.

Result: SAID accelerates DLLM inference up to 9.1x while maintaining comparable performance, as demonstrated on LLaDA-8B and LLaDA 1.5 models across benchmarks in math, coding, and knowledge tasks.

Conclusion: SAID effectively addresses the inefficiencies of DLLMs during inference by strategically reallocating computational resources according to token importance and confidence, resulting in significant speed improvements and maintaining high-quality generation.

Abstract: Diffusion large language models (DLLMs) enable non-autoregressive generation by iteratively denoising corrupted token sequences with bidirectional context. Despite their ability to update multiple positions in parallel, inference remains costly due to the many denoising steps required for high-quality generation. We propose SAID, a Scaffold-Aware Iterative Decoding framework that accelerates DLLMs by reallocating computation across tokens. SAID first spends denoising computation on scaffold tokens to establish the coarse semantic structure, and then completes predictable detail tokens with fewer steps. We further adapt SAID to block-wise diffusion decoding and introduce Confidence-Hierarchical Layered Generation (CHLG), which assigns additional steps only to low-confidence tokens. Experiments on LLaDA-8B and LLaDA 1.5 across math, coding, and knowledge benchmarks show that SAID significantly accelerates DLLM inference with a maximum speedup of 9.1x while maintaining competitive performance. Our code is publicly available: https://github.com/TH-AI-Lab-PKU/SAID.

</details>


### [109] [Probing Outcome-Level Resemblance and Mechanism-Level Alignment in LLM Risk Decisions: Evidence from the St. Petersburg Game](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04978)
*Chensong Huang,Changyu Chen,Chenwei Lin,Hanjia Lyu,Xian Xu,Jiebo Luo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper investigates large language models' (LLMs) decision-making in risk tasks using the St. Petersburg game as a testbed, showing that human-like outputs often lack consistent underlying mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to explore whether LLMs aligned in decision-making outcomes also align in decision-making mechanisms, utilizing the paradoxical nature of the St. Petersburg game where outcomes diverge from human behaviors.

Method: The authors tested 28 LLMs with structured prompts for variants of the St. Petersburg game, including modifications like truncation, repeated play, and human-perspective reasoning, while comparing base and instruction-tuned models.

Result: Models often produce finite bids resembling human-like outputs in the original game but exhibit mechanism-level differences. Controlled variants reveal shifts toward computationally rational behavior, while instruction tuning and human-cue prompts reduce visible issues but fail to fully align mechanisms.

Conclusion: Human-like decision outputs in LLMs may not reflect human-aligned reasoning processes. Future evaluations need to examine mechanism-level consistency, not just outcome-level alignment, for high-stakes decision-making scenarios.

Abstract: LLMs can appear cautious in risk decision-making tasks, yet cautious-looking outputs do not necessarily indicate alignment with human decision-making mechanisms. We investigate this distinction using the St. Petersburg game as a controlled testbed, a classical paradox in which the expected payoff is infinite, yet humans typically report low, finite willingness to pay. We evaluate 28 LLMs with a structured prompt suite that includes the original game; controlled decision variants that perturb truncation, repeated play, numeric endowment, and occupational identity; a human-perspective prompt that asks models to reason as human decision makers; and paired comparisons between base models and their instruction-tuned counterparts. In the original game, most models generate finite bids, creating the appearance of human-like risk behavior. However, this outcome-level resemblance masks substantial mechanism-level differences. The controlled variants reveal that rather than maintaining human-like behavior seen in the original game, models often shift to conditionally and computationally rational behavior. Human-cue prompting and instruction tuning often lower bids and reduce some visible pathologies, but most mechanism-level response patterns remain largely unchanged. These findings show that behavioral alignment in risk decision-making can be surface-level: LLMs may produce human-like risk decisions without exhibiting human-consistent mechanisms. High-stakes evaluations of LLM decision-making should therefore move beyond outcome similarity and examine whether the alignment is supported by mechanism-level consistency.

</details>


### [110] [DeliChess: A Multi-party Dialogue Dataset for Deliberation in Chess Puzzle Solving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04987)
*Xiaochen Zhu,Georgi Karadzhov,Tom Stafford,Andreas Vlachos*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces DeliChess, a dataset analyzing group deliberation dialogues to solve chess puzzles collaboratively, improving group accuracy through discussion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Group reasoning and decision-making processes are crucial but underexplored in structured, complex tasks, prompting the need for a specialized dataset.

Method: The authors created DeliChess, capturing 107 multi-party dialogues involving pre-and post-discussion choices, puzzle metadata, and analysis of probing utterances' impact on group performance.

Result: Deliberation improved group accuracy significantly, though probing utterances introduced variability without consistently enhancing performance.

Conclusion: DeliChess serves as a valuable resource for understanding group reasoning, dialogue dynamics, and strategic decision-making in collaborative contexts.

Abstract: Multi-party dialogue is a critical setting for studying collaborative reasoning and decision-making, yet existing datasets rarely focus on structured, in-depth complex reasoning tasks. We introduce DeliChess, a novel dataset of group deliberation dialogues in which participants collaboratively solve multiple-choice chess puzzles. Each group first completes the puzzle individually, then engages in a multi-party discussion before submitting a revised collective answer. The dataset includes 107 dialogues with full transcripts, pre- and post-discussion choices, and metadata on puzzle difficulty and move quality. We evaluate performance using three metrics based on chess engine evaluations, and find that deliberation significantly improves group accuracy. We further analyse the role of probing utterances (i.e., messages that elicit proposals, justifications, or strategic reflection) using a classifier trained on prior deliberation data. While probing makes group performance more variable after discussion, it does not consistently lead to better performance. Our dataset offers a rich testbed for modelling group reasoning, dialogue dynamics, and the resolution of differing perspectives and opinions in a well-defined strategic domain.

</details>


### [111] [GARL: Game-Theoretic Reinforcement Learning for Multi-Agent Strategic Prioritisation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05002)
*Yuxiao Ye,Yiwen Zhang,Huiyuan Xie,Yuqin Huang,Zhiyuan Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces GARL, a game-theoretic framework for multi-agent reinforcement learning, focused on strategic prioritization tasks, delivering improved task performance and leveraging smaller open-source LLMs effectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Strategic decision-making systems often rely on interaction policies rather than individual model capacity. The challenge lies in designing reinforcement signals that are structured and task-general. This paper aims to make policy optimization better aligned with the interaction structure in multi-agent systems.

Method: The proposed GARL framework treats strategic prioritization as a two-stage game involving resource allocation by competing agents followed by an arbiter's final ranking. Game-theoretic utilities derived from this process are transformed into role-specific reinforcement signals for optimizing policies.

Result: GARL efficiently improves ranking performance in tasks like legal issue prioritization, showcasing competitive performance by small open-source LLMs against strong closed-source LLMs and enhancing strategic decision-making abilities in the legal domain.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that a game-theoretic interaction structure can be effectively turned into reinforcement-learning objectives, offering a robust framework for improving multi-agent policy optimization in prioritization tasks.

Abstract: LLM-based multi-agent systems are increasingly used for strategic decision-making tasks. In such settings, performance depends not only on individual model capabilities, but also on the policies by which agents interact and adapt. Multi-agent reinforcement learning can optimise these interaction policies, but its reward design often remains task-specific and weakly grounded in interaction structure. To address this gap, we propose GARL, a GAme-theoretic Reinforcement Learning framework for multi-agent strategic prioritisation. GARL formalises strategic prioritisation as a two-stage game: competing agents first allocate strategic resources over a shared candidate set, and a higher-level arbiter then produces the final ranking. The resulting game-theoretic utilities are converted into role-specific reinforcement signals, allowing policy optimisation to be guided by structured interaction. We instantiate GARL on issues-in-dispute ranking, where the goal is to prioritise core issues in legal proceedings. Experiments show that GARL improves ranking performance, enables small open-source LLMs to become competitive with a strong closed-source LLM under the same candidate-ranking setting, and yields gains in legal-domain competence and broader strategic decision-making. Overall, GARL demonstrates how game-theoretic interaction structure can be turned into reinforcement-learning objectives, providing a principled approach to policy optimisation in multi-agent strategic prioritisation.

</details>


### [112] [DAR: Deontic Reasoning with Agentic Harnesses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05009)
*Guangyao Dou,William Jurayj,Nils Holzenberger,Benjamin Van Durme*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Deontic Agentic Reasoning (DAR) for enhancing LLM-based deontic reasoning by allowing model interaction with statutes on demand.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge where language models struggle with long, cross-referenced rulesets needed for deontic reasoning tasks.

Method: The authors propose DAR, an agentic reasoning framework, and evaluate it in multiple settings using hard datasets from DeonticBench.

Result: Agentic reasoning improves performance in some tasks, but issues arise with weaker models, particularly on numerical tasks, while consuming more tokens.

Conclusion: DAR shows promise in advancing deontic reasoning, though performance gains are variable across task types and model capabilities.

Abstract: Deontic reasoning is the task of answering questions by applying explicit rules and policies to case-specific facts, for example computing tax liability under a statute or determining the outcome of an immigration appeal. A key technical challenge for LLM-based deontic reasoning is that the relevant ruleset can be long and cross-referenced, so models may still fail to locate the rules needed for a particular reasoning step. We introduce Deontic Agentic Reasoning (DAR), an agentic reasoning setup in which the model interacts with the statutes on demand. We evaluate DAR under multiple harnesses on hard subsets of DeonticBench. Across these settings, we find that agentic harnesses can push the frontier on deontic reasoning tasks, but improvements are not uniform: weaker models often degrade on numerical tasks while consuming far more tokens.

</details>


### [113] [Depth-Attention: Cross-Layer Value Mixing for Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05014)
*Boyi Zeng,Yiqin Hao,Zitong Wang,Shixiang Song,He Li,Feichen Song,Yifan Liu,Ziwei He,Xinbing Wang,Zhouhan Lin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Depth-Attention enhances information flow across layers in Transformers without adding extra persistent inference state or parameters, yielding better model performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformers, while effective in self-attention across sequences, are limited in their ability to selectively reuse representations from earlier layers, especially for modern LLMs optimized with compressed cache methods.

Method: Depth-Attention integrates cross-layer selection within the attention module by enabling queries to attend over earlier layers' keys and values, mixing them into the value cache for reuse without parameter or persistent state increase.

Result: Depth-Attention improves perplexity and downstream accuracy on Qwen3-style decoders, achieving up to 2.3 accuracy points improvement over vanilla Transformers with minimal computational overhead.

Conclusion: Depth-Attention successfully facilitates deeper cross-layer interaction in Transformers, delivering state-of-the-art performance gains while maintaining efficiency.

Abstract: Self-attention selects information freely across the sequence, but across depth, Transformers merely add each layer's output to the residual stream, so later layers cannot selectively reuse earlier-layer representations. Recent cross-layer methods improve this flow but operate on hidden states outside attention, adding state beyond the key-value cache at inference--a cost that becomes increasingly salient as modern LLMs compress the cache with grouped-query and multi-head latent attention. We introduce Depth-Attention, which performs this selection inside the attention module itself: before a layer attends over the sequence, its query attends over the keys of earlier layers at the same token position and mixes their values into the value that self-attention then reads. Because Depth-Attention reuses the standard attention queries, keys, and value-cache slots, storing depth-mixed values in place of the original values, it adds no parameters and introduces no persistent inference state beyond the standard key-value cache--the same cache size as a vanilla decoder and less than hidden-state-based cross-layer methods. On Qwen3-style decoders at 1.5B and 3B parameters, Depth-Attention attains the lowest perplexity and the highest average downstream accuracy, improving over the vanilla Transformer by up to 2.3 accuracy points and surpassing strong cross-layer baselines in perplexity and average accuracy, while adding under 0.01% extra arithmetic FLOPs and no additional persistent inference state. The gains hold from 360M to 3B parameters and extend to looped Transformers.

</details>


### [114] [TaDA: Calibrated Probe Gating for Task-Domain LoRA Merging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05016)
*Huy Quoc To,Fuyi Li,Guangyan Huang,Ming Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a task-domain LoRA merging method, TaDA, which significantly improves performance on QA and image classification benchmarks compared to existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The challenge of combining task and domain LoRA adapters into a single model is underexplored, despite its practical benefits. Existing methods overlook layer-dependent asymmetries between task and domain adapters.

Method: TaDA introduces a training-free algorithm with probe-guided per-layer gating and subspace-aware merging, leveraging depth-specific asymmetries while discarding conflicting components.

Result: On six QA benchmarks, TaDA improves Llama-2-7B accuracy by 3.6 percentage points over DARE-TIES. On six image classification benchmarks, TaDA leads with 85.9% average accuracy and outperforms baselines on three benchmarks.

Conclusion: TaDA provides a lightweight, effective approach for merging task and domain adapters, offering state-of-the-art performance without inference overhead.

Abstract: Combining a task LoRA adapter with a domain LoRA adapter into a single unified model is a practical yet largely unexplored challenge. Existing methods treat both adapters as symmetric peers, applying uniform weights across all layers. We argue that task and domain adapters exhibit a consistent depth-dependent asymmetry across transformer architectures. Domain dominance increases with layer depth, while shallower layers retain stronger task-relevant signals. Motivated by this observation, we propose $\textbf{TaDA}$ ($\textbf{Ta}$sk-$\textbf{D}$omain LoR$\textbf{A}$ Merging), a training-free algorithm that exploits this structure through calibrated probe-guided per-layer gating and per-component subspace-aware merging. The gating assigns individual weights per layer and projection type using a probe signal proved invariant to adapter weight magnitude. The merging discards conflicting singular directions before combining the remaining components. $\textbf{TaDA}$ produces a standard rank-$r$ LoRA adapter with zero inference overhead. On six scientific QA benchmarks with Llama-2-7B, TaDA achieves an average accuracy of 0.452, outperforming DARE-TIES by +3.6 percentage points and obtaining the best result on all six benchmarks. On six image classification benchmarks with ViT-L/16, TaDA reaches 85.9\% average accuracy, improving over the strongest merging baseline while leading in three of the six individual benchmarks.

</details>


### [115] [Imbuing Large Language Models with Bidirectional Logic for Robust Chain Repair](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05030)
*Zehua Cheng,Wei Dai,Jiahao Sun,Thomas Lukasiewicz*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Teleological Reasoning Infilling (TRI), a method to mitigate compounding errors in autoregressive reasoning by using a novel infilling approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) struggle with error propagation in unidirectional reasoning tasks, leading to the need for a framework to handle mistakes surgically during reasoning processes.

Method: TRI reframes reasoning errors as fill-in-the-middle (FIM) tasks and uses a Prefix-Suffix-Middle sequence rearrangement alongside a two-stage training process involving supervised fine-tuning and preference optimization with symbolic verifiers.

Result: TRI achieves state-of-the-art performance on three reasoning benchmarks while reducing token usage per problem by 31.2%.

Conclusion: The TRI framework provides a robust method for repairing reasoning chains in LLMs, effectively improving reliability and efficiency in reasoning-based tasks.

Abstract: Autoregressive chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning in large language models (LLMs) is fundamentally forward-directed: each step conditions only on prior tokens. This unidirectional inductive bias renders even capable models susceptible to error snowballing, wherein a single logical or arithmetic mistake in an early step irreversibly corrupts the entire reasoning chain. We introduce Teleological Reasoning Infilling (\TRI{}), a training framework that endows decoder-only transformers with a native \emph{goal-conditioned bridging} capability. The key insight is to reframe erroneous reasoning segments as fill-in-the-middle (FIM) tasks: given a verified prefix premise $P$, a verified downstream milestone $S$, and the original query $Q$, the model must synthesise the logical bridge $M$ that connects $P$ to $S$ rigorously and completely. To achieve this with standard causal architectures, we introduce a Prefix-Suffix-Middle (PSM) sequence rearrangement with three non-overlapping sentinel tokens, enabling $M$ to attend to both $P$ and $S$ without any structural modification to the self-attention mechanism. Training proceeds in two stages: (i) Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on symbolically verified $(P, S, M)$ triples extracted from formal mathematics corpora, and (ii) Direct Preference Optimisation (DPO) with a deterministic symbolic verifier (Lean 4 / Python) as the sole reward oracle, eliminating LLM-judge sycophancy. At inference, TRI operates as a surgical repair module within a dual-system loop: a causal draft model generates an initial trace, the verifier pinpoints failures, and TRI infills only the damaged segment, leaving verified sections intact. Comprehensive experiments on three benchmarks demonstrate that TRI achieves state-of-the-art performance across all tasks, while reducing per-problem token expenditure by 31.2%.

</details>


### [116] [Boosting Self-Consistency with Ranking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05054)
*Maria Marina,Daniil Moskovskiy,Sergey Pletenev,Mikhail Salnikov,Alexander Panchenko,Viktor Moskvoretskii*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper proposes Ranking-Improved Self-Consistency (RISC), a method to enhance the answer-selection process in large language models by using a ranking-based approach instead of majority voting, leading to more accurate results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Majority voting in self-consistency often fails to identify correct answers already present among samples. The paper aims to address this limitation by improving answer-selection methods in large language models.

Method: The proposed RISC method reformulates answer selection as a ranking problem and employs a LambdaRank model to score candidate answers using five features: answer frequency, semantic centrality, and reasoning-trace consistency.

Result: RISC demonstrates a better accuracy-efficiency trade-off compared to standard self-consistency and other baselines across three datasets, excelling particularly in question answering benchmarks.

Conclusion: Learning to combine multiple informative signals during answer selection significantly improves the performance of large language models, as demonstrated by RISC's success with its feature-based scoring approach.

Abstract: Self-consistency improves large language models by sampling multiple reasoning paths and selecting the most frequent answer, but majority voting often fails to recover correct answers that are already present among the samples. We address this limitation with Ranking-Improved Self-Consistency (RISC), which reformulates answer selection in self-consistency as a ranking problem. Instead of relying on a single uncertainty or confidence signal, RISC uses a lightweight LambdaRank model to score candidate answers with five carefully designed features that capture answer frequency, semantic centrality, and reasoning-trace consistency. We evaluate RISC on three datasets under a range of test-time budgets. Across datasets, RISC consistently achieves a better accuracy-efficiency trade-off than standard self-consistency and strong baselines, with particularly large gains on question answering benchmarks. Further analysis shows that the proposed features are individually useful and, more importantly, complementary, highlighting the value of learning to combine multiple informative signals for test-time answer selection.

</details>


### [117] [Fast & Faithful Function Vectors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05079)
*Minh An Pham,Anton Segeler,Thomas Wiegand,Wojciech Samek,Sebastian Lapuschkin,Patrick Kahardipraja,Reduan Achtibat*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates the impact of choices in Function Vector (FV) design for Large Language Models (LLMs), focusing on attention head selection and steering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To better understand and optimize how Function Vectors (FVs) are defined and used in steering LLMs, as their design choices are currently underexplored.

Method: The study varies FV definitions along two degrees: attention head selection (using Layer-wise Relevance Propagation) and steering methods (comparing distributed versus aggregated application).

Result: Gradient-based attributions with LRP improve efficiency and accuracy in attention head selection, and distributed steering outperforms simple aggregation in accuracy.

Conclusion: Careful design of FV definitions, specifically using gradient-based attributions with LRP for head selection and distributed steering methods, can significantly enhance LLM performance.

Abstract: Function vectors (FVs) are task representations elicited during in-context learning that can be used to steer Large Language Models (LLMs). However, design choices in their formulation remain underexplored. In this work, we study the impact of varying FV definitions for instructions along two degrees of freedom: attention head selection and steering. For head selection, using gradient-based attributions with Layer-wise Relevance Propagation (LRP) substantially improves efficiency as well as accuracy. For FV steering, applying it in a distributed manner yields a higher accuracy compared to simple aggregation. Our code is publicly available.

</details>


### [118] [Automatic Generation of Titles for Research Papers Using Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05085)
*Tohida Rehman,Debarshi Kumar Sanyal,Samiran Chattopadhyay*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method to generate research paper titles from abstracts, leveraging pre-trained large language models and new datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Title selection for academic papers can be challenging, and the authors aim to facilitate this process with automated title generation using AI models.

Method: The method involves using datasets including CSPubSum, LREC-COLING-2024, and a new dataset SpringerSSAT to experiment with models like PEGASUS-large, LLaMA-3-8B, and GPT-3.5-turbo under different setups. Evaluation metrics include ROUGE, METEOR, and BERTScore variants.

Result: The fine-tuned PEGASUS-large model outperforms others for generating accurate titles, while ChatGPT demonstrates creativity in title generation.

Conclusion: AI-generated paper titles are both effective and reliable, with PEGASUS-large leading in performance and ChatGPT showing potential for creative applications.

Abstract: The title of a research paper conveys its primary idea and, occasionally, its conclusions in a clear and concise manner. Choosing an appropriate title is often challenging, and automated title generation can assist authors in this task. In this work, we propose a technique to generate paper titles from abstracts using open-weight pre-trained and large language models. We use the CSPubSum and LREC-COLING-2024 datasets and introduce a new dataset, SpringerSSAT, curated from four Springer journals in the social sciences. Additionally, we use GPT-3.5-turbo in a zero-shot setting to generate titles. Model performance is evaluated with ROUGE, METEOR, MoverScore, BERTScore, and SciBERTScore metrics. Our experiments show that fine-tuned PEGASUS-large outperforms other models, including fine-tuned LLaMA-3-8B and zero-shot GPT-3.5-turbo, across most metrics. We further demonstrate that ChatGPT can generate creative paper titles. Overall, AI-generated titles are generally appropriate and reliable.

</details>


### [119] [Light or Full Verb? A Minimal-Pair Dataset for Probing Phraseological Competence in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05087)
*Francesca Franzon,Nicolas Rosàs Gómez,Leo Wanner*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether language models distinguish between the dual roles of English verbs like 'make' (as a light-verb vs. full lexical predicate). It provides a controlled dataset and shows that models can differentiate these uses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand if modern language models can distinguish the subtle differences in usage of frequent English verbs when used as light-verbs versus full lexical predicates.

Method: The authors created a large-scale dataset containing English sentence series, where each series varied minimally but demonstrated different uses of the same verb. Probing experiments were conducted to analyze how language models responded across these distinctions.

Result: Language models successfully differentiated between light-verb and full-verb uses of English verbs, even in minimal contexts, and exhibited clear patterns in relation to object types.

Conclusion: Language models demonstrate an ability to distinguish usage patterns for dual-role verbs. The provided dataset and framework offer reusable tools for extending the study to broader contexts, more verbs, and other languages.

Abstract: Frequent English verbs such as 'have' and 'make' can function either as collocates in light-verb constructions or as full lexical predicates, as in 'make a decision' vs. 'make a cake'. Whether language models represent this distinction remains unclear. We introduce a large-scale controlled dataset of minimally varying English sentence series in which the same context contains the same verb in light-verb and full-verb uses. Two probing experiments show that language models differentiate between these uses even in minimal contexts and exhibit separable patterns across object types. We release the dataset, generation code, and materials as a reusable resource. The framework supports extensions to broader contexts, additional verbs, and other languages.

</details>


### [120] [Arithmetic Pedagogy for Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05106)
*Andhika Bernard Lumbantobing,Hokky Situngkir*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper applies pedagogical methods from human arithmetic teaching, particularly the Indonesian GASING approach, to train a small language model for arithmetic reasoning, achieving strong performance with limited model size.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The aim is to improve small-scale language model performance in arithmetic reasoning by exploring the pedagogical methods used in human teaching, thereby making training more efficient and accessible.

Method: The authors adapted the GASING method into a computational procedure expressed as Chain-of-Thought (CoT) supervision. They trained an 86M-parameter GPT-2 model with Indonesian TOBA tokenization on this CoT-annotated data using next-token prediction only.

Result: The model underwent three learning phases and achieved over 80% accuracy on held-out arithmetic problems. It also demonstrated competitive performance compared to larger models, achieving economical and efficient arithmetic reasoning capabilities.

Conclusion: Targeted, pedagogy-based training approaches can significantly boost arithmetic reasoning in small language models, showing that scale is not the only pathway to enhanced capabilities.

Abstract: We investigate whether methods of human mathematics pedagogy can guide the training of language models toward arithmetic reasoning. Building on the GASING method -- an Indonesian pedagogy that solves basic arithmetic through a left-to-right procedure aligned with the causal order of token generation -- we operationalize each operation as a computational procedure whose execution trace is serialized into natural-language Chain-of-Thought (CoT) supervision. A small GPT-2 decoder (86M parameters) with a syllabic-agglutinative TOBA tokenizer for Indonesian is trained from scratch on this data using only a next-token prediction objective, without reinforcement learning or reward-based optimization. Monitoring training reveals three distinct learning phases, and mechanistic analyses -- attention-masking interventions on the CoT information graph, residual-stream probing, and logit-lens inspection -- show that the model first internalizes a procedural pathway and subsequently develops an associative, ``mental-arithmetic'' capacity that retrieves intermediate results without explicit step-by-step computation. The trained model reaches over 80% accuracy on held-out problems and attains competitive performance against substantially larger language models, indicating that targeted, pedagogically grounded training can yield strong and economical arithmetic capability at small scale.

</details>


### [121] [Evaluating Large Language Models in Dynamic Clinical Decision-Making with Standardized Patient Cases](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05112)
*Cheng Liang,Pengcheng Qiu,Ya Zhang,Yanfeng Wang,Chaoyi Wu,Weidi Xie*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents MedSP1000, a clinical-agent evaluation benchmark derived from standardized patient (SP) teaching cases, revealing that current large language models (LLMs) are inadequate for reliable clinical practice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap in static, single-turn benchmarks by creating a realistic and dynamic evaluation framework for clinical agents, inspired by standardized patient (SP) practices in medical education.

Method: The study converts 1,638 peer-reviewed standardized patient (SP) teaching cases into MedSP1000, an SP-derived interactive benchmark with defined patient scripts, contexts, and expert-validated rubrics. LLMs were evaluated through closed-loop simulation and scored against these rubrics.

Result: General-purpose and medically specialized LLMs performed poorly, with GPT-5.5 completing only 60.4% of rubric criteria and a medically specialized model achieving just 40.0%. Increased computational resources did not improve performance.

Conclusion: Current LLMs, including those tailored for medicine, are not reliable for clinical practice. MedSP1000 highlights the clinical shortcomings of LLMs that static benchmarks fail to identify.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly proposed as clinical agents, yet static, single-turn benchmarks cannot capture how a model dynamically delivers care across an encounter: gathering information, planning treatment, and adapting longitudinal management across successive patient states. Medical education has long addressed an analogous challenge through standardized patients (SPs): trained actors who consistently portray clinical cases, enabling realistic practice and objective, scripted assessment. Here we introduce MedSP1000, an SP-derived interactive benchmark for clinical-agent evaluation, including 1,638 SP cases with 24,602 trajectory-level peer-reviewed rubrics. MedSP1000 converts peer-reviewed SP teaching cases into executable scenarios with defined SP case scripts, clinical environment contexts, and human-validated structured rubric. In each simulation evaluation run, a clinical agent interacts in closed loop with a patient agent and an environment controller, and its behaviour is scored throughout the encounter against expert criteria specified in the original materials. Applying MedSP1000 to a range of general-purpose and medically specialized LLMs, we find that performance on static benchmarks does not reliably translate to such educational scenarios. The best-performing model, GPT-5.5, completes only 60.4% of expert-defined rubric items, whereas the strongest medically specialized model reaches 40.0%; increasing test-time compute produces no measurable gain. These results suggest that current LLMs, including agentic systems tuned for medicine, are not yet reliable enough to be safely integrated into actual clinical practice. More broadly, MedSP1000 shows how process-level, SP-style evaluation can reveal clinically relevant failure modes that single-turn benchmarks miss.

</details>


### [122] [Self-Evaluation Is Already There: Eliciting Latent Judge Calibration in Base LLMs with Minimal Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05122)
*XiuYu Zhang,Yi Shan,Junfeng Fang,Zhenkai Liang*

Main category: cs.CL
