Motivation: To bridge the performance gap between open-source RFMs and commercial models, understand underlying limitations of existing RFM frameworks, and propose enhancements for better accuracy and usability in relational database predictions.

Method: The paper examines RT's limitations in relation-level ICL and sparse label coverage. It also probes pre-training regimes (synthetic-only vs. real-data driven) and formulates a dual-stage ICL architecture enhanced by relational backbone integration and homophily-aware pre-training strategies.

Result: OpenRFM is introduced with improved pre-training and architecture, leading to a 30% better average task performance than RT and exceeding the commercial model KumoRFMv1 in evaluations.

Conclusion: The novel improvements in architecture and pre-training strategies significantly reduce the gap between open and commercial RFMs, providing a simpler yet highly effective solution for relational database predictions in OpenRFM.

Abstract: Relational Foundation Models (RFMs) promise a single pre-trained predictor that, given any relational database, returns predictions in one forward pass via relational in-context learning (ICL). Yet a substantial gap separates open RFMs from their commercial counterparts, and the origin of this gap has not been systematically understood. We dissect a representative framework, the Relational Transformer (RT), from two perspectives. Model side: we show that RT performs relation-level ICL, and a kernel regression view shows it fails when sparse label-cell coverage yields an underdetermined regression. Data side: we ablate RT's pre-training source and find that existing synthetic-only pre-training and in-distribution pre-training drive the same architecture into different regimes, lazy vs. feature-learning. Probing this gap reveals that the missing ingredient is a support-identifiable relational latent in the label-generation process. These two diagnoses translate into (1) a dual-stage ICL architecture that combines the relational backbone with a batch-level ICL layer lifted from a pre-trained tabular foundation model to overcome relation-level label scarcity, and (2) a homophily-aware synthetic plus continual real-data pre-training mixture, augmented with a prototype-based regularization. These choices define OpenRFM, a simple yet effective RFM that improves average task performance by approximately 30% over the RT backbone and surpasses the commercial model KumoRFMv1 on a large set of evaluation tasks.

</details>


### [297] [Measuring What Matters: Synthetic Benchmarks for Concept Bottleneck Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04326)
*Julian Skirzynski,Harry Cheon,Shreyas Kadekodi,Meredith Stewart,Berk Ustun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces synthetic benchmarks tailored for evaluating concept bottleneck models, focusing on decision support and automation tasks, with controlled factors like data modality and annotation quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Limited availability of datasets with concept labels hinders understanding and performance analysis of concept bottleneck models.

Method: Develop synthetic benchmarks that generate labeled datasets, allowing control over factors that influence performance, such as concept choice and annotation completeness.

Result: Benchmarks provide a systematic framework for evaluating, diagnosing failure modes, and guiding further testing of concept bottleneck models.

Conclusion: Synthetic benchmarks are effective tools for advancing research and practical applications of concept bottleneck models by addressing key challenges like interpretability and failure mode analysis.

Abstract: Concept bottleneck models predict outcomes from high-level concepts detected in inputs. Although concepts provide a simple way to reap benefits from interpretability, very few datasets include concept labels. This limits researchers' ability to determine which problems are suitable for these models, isolate the factors that drive their performance or lead to failures, or uncover which algorithms perform well. In this paper, we develop synthetic benchmarks for concept-bottleneck models, focusing on their two main use cases: decision support, in which models assist humans in making better decisions, and automation, in which models handle routine tasks without supervision. Our benchmarks can generate labeled datasets while controlling for properties that affect performance, including data modality, concept choice, annotation quality, and completeness. We demonstrate how the benchmarks can be used to evaluate representative classes of concept bottleneck models. Our demonstrations show how the benchmarks can diagnose failure modes and guide follow-up testing.

</details>


### [298] [A Geometric Characterization of the Stationary Plateau for Two-Layer Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04327)
*Tian Ding,Dawei Li,Ruoyu Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper explores the geometry of stationary plateaus in the loss landscape of two-layer neural networks, particularly focusing on neuron splitting and its impact on stationary points.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how neuron splitting in neural networks influences the geometry of stationary plateaus and its consequences on local minima or saddle points.

Method: The authors analyze the phenomenon of neuron splitting using the concept of the "inner Hessian" matrix, studying its role in defining the local geometry of plateaus.

Result: Splitting a local minimum can lead to a mix of minima and saddles or entirely form an all-saddle plateau, while splitting a saddle point always results in a saddle plateau.

Conclusion: The findings provide unified insights into how width expansion and reparameterization impact the stationary loss landscape of neural networks, offering a better understanding of their geometric structure.

Abstract: We investigate the geometric structure of stationary plateaus that arise in the loss landscape of two-layer neural networks with smooth activation functions. We focus on the phenomenon of "neuron splitting" where duplicating a hidden neuron yields an affine set of stationary points in a wider network. We provide a comprehensive classification of all stationary points on these plateaus, determining under what conditions they constitute local minima or saddle points. Our characterization hinges on a per-neuron curvature object we term the "inner Hessian" matrix. Our analysis reveals that the definiteness of the inner Hessian and the choice of splitting coefficients jointly dictate the local geometry of the plateau. We show that "splitting" a local minimum can yield either a mixture of local minima and saddles or an all-saddle plateau, with a concrete sure-saddle region identified under mild assumptions. In contrast, splitting a saddle point always produces a plateau of saddle points. Our results unify and extend prior landscape analyses, elucidating when and how model expansion preserves or alters the nature of stationary points. These findings offer new geometric insights into the effects of width expansion and reparameterization in neural networks.

</details>


### [299] [Policy Gradient for Continuous-Time Robust Markov Decision Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04335)
*Tanya Veeravalli,David M. Bossens,Atsushi Nitanda*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes and analyzes policy gradient algorithms for continuous-time robust Markov decision processes (RMDPs), achieving efficient optimization methods and sample complexities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the need for robust reinforcement learning agents that can handle continuous-time dynamics with performance guarantees under worst-case conditions.

Method: The study derives policy and adversarial gradients using pathwise and adjoint-based approaches, introduces double-loop optimizers for linear convergence, and develops mean-field optimizers for distributional optimization. Analytical tools are also developed for undiscounted total cost MDPs.

Result: Double-loop optimizers achieve linear convergence and a sample complexity of $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\frac{1}{ε^2})$, while mean-field optimizers achieve a $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\frac{1}{K})$ convergence rate and $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\frac{N^2}{ε})$ sample complexity. Experiments validate the effectiveness of the proposed methods.

Conclusion: The introduced algorithms and optimizers enable more efficient and robust policy learning in continuous-time RMDPs, extending the applicability of robust RL frameworks.

Abstract: The framework of robust Markov decision processes (RMDPs) allows the design of reinforcement learning agents that satisfy performance guarantees under worst-case transition dynamics. Traditional RMDPs consider discrete-time dynamics and recently, sample-efficient policy gradient algorithms have been considered in this context. This paper investigates policy gradient algorithms within a continuous-time RMDP framework. Policy gradients and adversarial gradients are derived using pathwise and adjoint-based formulas for stochastic and ordinary differential equations. We propose double-loop optimisers to obtain linear convergence in the oracle-based setting and an $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\frac{1}{ε^2})$ sample complexity in the sample-based setting in an analysis which also derives novel tools for the framework of undiscounted total cost MDPs. Additionally, we propose mean-field optimisers as distributional optimisers with an $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\frac{1}{K})$ oracle-based convergence rate and an $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\frac{N^2}ε)$ sample complexity under $N$-particle approximation. The effectiveness of continuous-time policy gradient algorithms is confirmed for both optimisers on continuous-time RMDPs with neural ordinary differential equation dynamics.

</details>


### [300] [Federated Learning for Multi-Center Sepsis Early Prediction with Privacy-Preserving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04338)
*Xixi Tian,Di Wu,Xiang Liu,Yiziting Zhu,Yujie Li,Xin Shu,Bin Yi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The study examines federated learning (FL) for sepsis prediction across medical centers, showing comparable accuracy to centralized models while ensuring privacy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges of privacy-sensitive and distributed medical data in creating centralized sepsis prediction models by evaluating federated learning as an alternative.

Method: The researchers used 648 clinically screened samples from three hospitals in China. A centralized model was established as a baseline and compared with a horizontal FL framework for distributed training. Privacy and security of FL were analyzed regarding data reconstruction attacks.

Result: FL-based models achieved accuracy comparable to centralized models, preventing privacy leakage and demonstrating resistance to data reconstruction attacks.

Conclusion: Federated learning is a practical, secure, and reliable method for privacy-preserving multi-center medical collaboration, validated through its application in sepsis prediction.

Abstract: Privacy-sensitive and distributed characteristics of multi-center medical data bring severe obstacles to centralized modeling for accurate early prediction of sepsis. Federated learning (FL) has attracted growing attention as a promising framework for collaborative model development, as it allows multiple institutions to jointly train predictive models without directly sharing or centralizing raw data. Nevertheless, its practical performance, robustness, and privacy-preserving benefits remain insufficiently evaluated using real-world clinical datasets. To bridge this gap, this study systematically examines the application of federated learning to multi-center sepsis prediction. The experimental dataset consists of 648 clinically screened samples collected from three tertiary hospitals in China, with rigorous inclusion and exclusion criteria. We establish a centralized training paradigm as the performance baseline, and then implement a horizontal federated learning framework for distributed collaborative modeling. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the federated learning-based model achieves highly comparable prediction accuracy to the centralized counterpart, while fundamentally avoiding privacy leakage. Further privacy security analysis verifies that malicious attackers cannot reconstruct the original patient data from the transmitted model parameters, indicating strong resistance against data reconstruction attacks. This work not only validates the practicality and security of federated learning in clinical sepsis prediction, but also provides a reliable and feasible solution for privacy-preserving multi-center medical collaboration.

</details>


### [301] [Literature-Guided Minimax Optimization of Virtual Epilepsy Neurostimulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04339)
*Cathy Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a robust optimization framework leveraging computational epilepsy models, literature-driven insights, and black-box optimization to improve worst-case seizure control outcomes in virtual patients.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing neuromodulation therapies often optimize for average outcomes, potentially neglecting the worst-performing scenarios, which can be critical for personalized treatment.

Method: A minimax pipeline integrates literature-guided hypothesis extraction, computational epilepsy simulations via The Virtual Brain (TVB) Epileptor, and large-language-model-powered optimization to identify patient-specific neuromodulation protocols.

Result: Intrinsic model-control parameters improved the worst-case reward by 39.8%, while external-stimulation protocols yielded less improvement (1.7%) and showed limited efficacy across a virtual cohort.

Conclusion: This research serves as a proof of concept for designing robust, patient-specific neurostimulation strategies based on computational insights, though it is not yet clinically validated.

Abstract: Computational models of epilepsy promise patient-specific treatment design, but most optimization workflows still search for parameters that perform well on average. In neuromodulation, this is a weak target: a protocol that improves the mean response can still fail in the patient whose network is least tolerant to stimulation. We present a literature-guided minimax pipeline that couples PubMed-scale hypothesis extraction, The Virtual Brain (TVB) Epileptor simulations, and large-language-model-guided black-box optimization. The optimizer proposes either intrinsic model-control parameters or clinically interpretable external-stimulation protocols; TVB evaluates each proposal across sampled virtual patients; and the objective maximizes worst-case reward, defined as the negative variance of simulated seizure activity. In the intrinsic model-control experiment, the best archived parameter set improved worst-case reward from -0.5285 to -0.3182, a 39.8% gain over baseline. The clinical-style external-stimulation search produced a much smaller worst-case improvement (1.7%), and a 20-patient virtual cohort showed no aggregate benefit (p=0.9019), despite a 55% responder rate and a positive temporal-lobe subgroup signal. The study should be read as an in silico proof of concept for robust, literature-aware neurostimulation design, not as clinical evidence.

</details>


### [302] [Expectations vs. Realities: The Cost of MSE-Optimal Forecasting Under Conditional Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04342)
*Riku Green,Zahraa S. Abdallah,Telmo M Silva Filho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study critiques MSE-based evaluation for multi-step time series forecasting, highlighting a trade-off between accuracy and marginal realism under conditional uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation methods like MSE fail to adequately address conditional uncertainty in long-horizon forecasting, leading to misrepresentative predictors.

Method: The paper introduces a formal framework called the conditional uncertainty gap and uses theoretical proof alongside experiments on controlled systems and nine real-world benchmarks to expose the trade-off between point accuracy and marginal realism.

Result: The study establishes an accuracy-realism trade-off and observes that small sacrifices in MSE (≤5%) yield significant gains in marginal realism (median improvement of 17.3%).

Conclusion: The work highlights a structural limitation of MSE-based evaluation, urging a paradigm shift towards balancing accuracy and realism in forecasting via method and inference strategy selection.

Abstract: Multi-step time series forecasting (MSF) is commonly evaluated using point-wise error metrics such as mean squared error (MSE), implicitly treating the conditional mean as a sufficient target. We show that this can be misleading under conditional uncertainty, where the conditional expectation becomes unrepresentative of typical realized values at longer horizons. We formalize this effect through a conditional uncertainty gap and prove that whenever this gap is nonzero, no deterministic predictor can simultaneously minimize MSE and match the marginal distribution of realized futures. This establishes a fundamental, model-agnostic trade-off between point accuracy and marginal realism in MSF evaluation. Using controlled stochastic dynamical systems and nine real-world forecasting benchmarks, we empirically characterize the resulting accuracy--realism frontier and \textbf{quantify the practical cost of MSE-only model selection}. As conditional uncertainty increases with forecast horizon, the attainable set expands into a pronounced Pareto front, separating MSE-optimal but under-dispersed predictors from methods that trade accuracy for realistic marginal variability. \textbf{Across benchmarks, we find that small relaxations in MSE ($\boldsymbol{\le 5\%}$) frequently unlock disproportionate gains in marginal realism, with median improvements of $\mathbf{17.3\%}$ and gains exceeding $\mathbf{30\%}$ in some datasets.} We further show that common forecasting strategies systematically occupy different regions of this frontier: direct multi-output predictors concentrate near the accuracy-optimal extreme, while recursive strategies and sample-based inference favors marginal realism. Together, these results expose a structural failure mode of MSE-based evaluation in long-horizon forecasting and recast strategy and inference selection as navigation of an unavoidable accuracy--realism trade-off.

</details>


### [303] [MeshTok: Efficient Multi-Scale Tokenization for Scalable PDE Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04366)
*Yanshun Zhao,Xiaoyu Peng,Jiamin Jiang,Congcong Zhu,Jingrun Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MeshTok is a framework for adaptive tokenization and sequence modeling inspired by adaptive mesh refinement, enhancing Transformers for complex PDE modeling with improved efficiency and accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional patchified Transformers struggle to efficiently tokenize and process heterogeneous solutions to complex PDEs due to their uniform spatial partitioning.

Method: MeshTok introduces an adaptive mesh refinement-inspired tokenization that selectively refines areas with complex features, producing multiscale tokens processed in a unified Transformer model.

Result: Experiments show MeshTok achieves better efficiency-accuracy trade-offs than uniform-grid baselines across several PDE families and benchmark datasets.

Conclusion: MeshTok demonstrates the benefits of adaptive multiscale tokenization as a scalable and effective approach for neural PDE modeling.

Abstract: Conventional patchified Transformers operate on uniform spatial partitions, distributing computational effort evenly across the domain irrespective of local features. This inflexible tokenization scheme is inherently limited in its ability to efficiently represent and process solutions to complex PDEs. To address this, we propose MeshTok, an adaptive mesh refinement (AMR)-inspired tokenization and sequence modeling framework. This method selectively refines spatial regions exhibiting sharp gradients, transient features, or multiscale structures, generating a heterogeneous set of multiscale tokens defined on a fixed simulation grid. These tokens are processed within a unified Transformer sequence, enabling the model to simultaneously capture coarse-grained global context and fine-grained local details without requiring specialized architectural components. Although adaptive refinement moderately increases token count, it promotes a more targeted allocation of computational resources to physically informative regions, which we view as a practical inductive bias rather than a formal optimality guarantee. Experimental evaluations across multiple PDE families and benchmark datasets demonstrate that MeshTok consistently improves the efficiency-accuracy trade-off compared to uniform-grid baselines. This suggests adaptive multiscale tokenization as a scalable and generalizable design principle for neural PDE modeling. Code is available at https://github.com/SCAILab-USTC/MeshTok.

</details>


### [304] [From Symbolic to Geometric: Enabling Spatial Reasoning in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04381)
*Chen Chu,Bita Azarijoo,Li Xiong,Khurram Shafique,Cyrus Shahabi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces the Spatial Language Model (SLM), a multimodal large language model that integrates geometric spatial reasoning with natural language processing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models struggle with true geometric spatial reasoning because they rely on symbolic pattern matching instead of continuous spatial representations and geometric computation.

Method: The authors propose the Spatial Language Model (SLM) that uses learned spatial representations and is trained with a new Spatial Instruction Dataset. They also introduce a SpatialEval benchmark to measure spatial reasoning capability across various tasks.

Result: SLM significantly outperforms LLM-based methods relying on symbolic reasoning, showing its effectiveness in integrating geometric spatial representations for spatial reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: Introducing geometric spatial reasoning as a core capability in language models improves their performance on spatial reasoning tasks, and SLM is a significant advancement in this domain.

Abstract: Recent large language models (LLMs) often appear to exhibit spatial reasoning ability; however, this capability is largely \emph{symbolic}, arising from pattern matching over spatial language rather than true \emph{geometric} reasoning over space. Because LLMs operate on discrete tokens, they lack native support for continuous spatial representations, explicit geometric computation, and structured spatial operators. To address this limitation, we introduce the \emph{Spatial Language Model (SLM)}, the first multimodal LLM that treats location information as a first-class modality and enables geometric spatial reasoning within the model's inference process. SLM directly operates on learned spatial representations rather than textual descriptions of spatial relations. To support effective training, we construct a \emph{Spatial Instruction Dataset} that aligns spatial representations, atomic geometric operations, and natural language instructions. We further propose a new benchmark named \emph{SpatialEval}, which is designed to evaluate spatial reasoning across attributes, distance, topology, and relative-position tasks. Extensive experiments show that SLM significantly outperforms existing LLM-based approaches that rely on symbolic reasoning via prompt engineering or textual abstraction, demonstrating the benefits of integrating geometric spatial representations for robust spatial reasoning.
  Our instruction dataset, evaluation benchmark, model training codes, and models' checkpoints can be found at:
  \hyperlink{https://github.com/chuchen2017/SLM}{https://github.com/chuchen2017/SLM}.

</details>


### [305] [Shortcomings and capacities of real-constrained neural networks in complex spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04390)
*Andrew Gracyk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper evaluates the ratio of storage capacities by comparing real versus complex pre-activations in machine learning models, using advanced mathematical formulas and manifold integration techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study addresses the need to understand storage capacity differences between real and complex pre-activations in complex hypothesis classes, aiming to refine the theoretical understanding of machine learning model capacity.

Method: The method involves comparing Gardner volumes at critical storage capacities and leveraging advanced mathematical tools like the Harish-Chandra-Itzykson-Zuber (HCIZ) formula, along with integration over compact manifolds using the Weyl integration formula and the Haar measure.

Result: The analysis reveals an asymptotic ratio between storage capacities for real and complex pre-activations, accompanied by a more robust approximation of this ratio due to the application of the HCIZ formula.

Conclusion: Employing nonstandard mathematical techniques enhances the robustness of the analysis, providing deeper insights into storage capacity comparisons in complex hypothesis classes.

Abstract: We find the asymptotic ratio between the storage capacities when enforcing real pre-activations in a complex hypothesis class as opposed to complex ones in the same class. Our methods depend on Gardner volume comparisons at critical capacity. Our proof relies on an application of the Harish-Chandra-Itzykson-Zuber (HCIZ) formula, nonstandard in literature. With the HCIZ formula, we may obtain a more robust approximation for the final asymptotic ratio. This strategy is applicable to our work specifically since we integrate over the unitary and orthogonal compact manifolds, facilitated via the Weyl integration formula and the Haar measure.

</details>


### [306] [Physics-Informed Neural Network Modeling of Biodegradable Contaminant Transport through GCL/SL Composite Liners](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04392)
*Dong Li,Yapeng Cao,Haiping Zhao,Shutong Han*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study proposes a new H-PINN framework for accurately modeling and predicting contaminant transport in composite liner systems, reducing errors and improving predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Contaminant transport through composite liner systems is complex and demands accurate predictive models for environmental safety. Conventional methods face challenges in accuracy and computational stability.

Method: The study compares two physics-informed neural network (PINN) formulations: Std-PINN and H-PINN. H-PINN incorporates hard constraints into trial solutions, improving performance with boundary and initial conditions. It uses optimization and tanh activation for predictive accuracy.

Result: H-PINN significantly outperforms Std-PINN by reducing Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and Mean Relative Error (MRE), achieving consistent and accurate predictions. It also demonstrates robust inverse modeling capabilities for parameter identification.

Conclusion: The H-PINN framework shows promise for environmental applications, offering precise and stable contaminant transport predictions and effective inverse modeling for parameter estimation.

Abstract: This study develops a two-domain physics-informed neural network framework for contaminant transport through a GCL/SL composite liner system, in which the thin GCL layer is treated using a steady-state advection-dispersion-biodegradation formulation and the underlying soil liner is modeled as a transient transport domain. Two formulations are evaluated against analytical and finite-element reference solutions under different leachate-head conditions: a standard PINN with soft constraint enforcement (Std-PINN) and a hard-constrained PINN (H-PINN), in which selected boundary and initial conditions are embedded directly into the trial solutions. The Std-PINN captures the overall breakthrough behavior but shows larger errors during the early transport stage, particularly under higher leachate heads where advective transport becomes more pronounced. The H-PINN reduces the optimization burden associated with penalty-based constraint enforcement and provides more accurate and stable concentration predictions, lowering the MAE from approximately 0.058-0.067 for the Std-PINN to about 0.011-0.023 for the H-PINN, while reducing the MRE from approximately 9.10%-19.16% to about 2.08%-3.14%. Parametric analyses confirm that the H-PINN with the tanh activation function and an optimized network structure provides the best predictive accuracy. The H-PINN is further extended to inverse modeling for identifying the SL degradation half-life from limited concentration observations, showing reliable convergence toward prescribed values and acceptable robustness under low-to-moderate observation noise.

</details>


### [307] [DPDL: Towards Differential Privacy Preservation in Decentralized Stochastic Learning on Non-IID Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04399)
*Yunsheng Yuan,Xue Xiao,Lina Wang,Feng Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a decentralized learning algorithm, DPDL, that uses differential privacy to protect data privacy while managing non-IID data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address privacy risks and challenges arising in decentralized learning setups where agents operate on non-identical datasets and lack a central server.

Method: DPDL incorporates a differential privacy mechanism by perturbing cross-gradient information with Gaussian noise. It also uses cosine similarity to calibrate gradients for model updates.

Result: Theoretical analysis ensures privacy preservation and linear training speedup. Extensive experiments demonstrate privacy attack defense and accurate model training.

Conclusion: DPDL offers a robust decentralized learning framework combining differential privacy with effective training even under non-IID data conditions.

Abstract: In the paradigm of decentralized learning, a group of agents collaborate to train a global model using distributed datasets without a central server. Although the power of collaboration has been verified by many state-of-the-art studies, it entails extensive gradient information exchanging among the agents and thus induces high risk of privacy leakage for the individual agents. Moreover, in real-world applications, the training data are usually non-identically and independently distributed across the agents, inducing more challenges to enable privacy-preserved decentralized learning. To address these issues, we propose a privacy-preserved decentralized learning algorithm with non-IID data, DPDL, which leverages the notion of Differential Privacy (DP) in cross-gradient aggregation through a similarity-based calibration technique. Specifically, in each round, each agent perturbs the cross-gradients (i.e., the derivatives of its neighbors' local model in its private local data) by Gaussian noise mechanism before sharing them with its neighbors; it then adopt cosine similarity to calibrate the received perturbed cross-gradients such that the aggregation of the calibrated cross-gradients can be utilized to effectively update local model in a momentum-like manner. Our rigorous theoretical analysis not only reveals the minimum noise level required to achieve a specific level of privacy preservation, but also illustrates that our algorithm still achieves a linear speedup in training with non-IID data. We finally conduct extensive experiments on real-world dataset to validate the effectiveness of our algorithm in defending privacy attacks and in training accurate models.

</details>


### [308] [TANDEM: Bi-Level Data Mixture Optimization with Twin Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04401)
*Jiaxing Wang,Deping Xiang,Jin Xu,Mingyang Yi,Guoqiang Gong,Zicheng Zhang,Haoran Li,Pengzhang Liu,Zhen Chen,Ke Zhang,Ju Fan,Qixiang Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces TANDEM, a twin-network method for optimizing domain-specific data mixture ratios in large language models (LLMs), showing superior performance and wide applicability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of optimizing domain-specific training data mixture ratios in LLMs to improve model performance.

Method: Propose TANDEM, a twin-network system with a proxy model and dynamically updated reference model to optimize mixture ratios via a simplified penalized bi-level optimization process.

Result: TANDEM provides improved data efficacy and performance optimization across various scenarios, including data-restricted contexts and supervised fine-tuning.

Conclusion: TANDEM outperforms prior methods, offers theoretical guarantees, and introduces new settings for studying domain reweighting, validating its effectiveness through extensive experiments.

Abstract: The capabilities of large language models (LLMs) significantly depend on training data drawn from various domains. Optimizing domain-specific mixture ratios can be modeled as a bi-level optimization problem, which we simplify into a single-level penalized form and solve with twin networks: a proxy model trained on primary data and a dynamically updated reference model trained with additional data. Our proposed method, Twin Networks for bi-level DatA mixturE optiMization (TANDEM), measures the data efficacy through the difference between the twin models and up-weights domains that benefit more from the additional data. TANDEM provides theoretical guarantees and wider applicability, compared to prior approaches. Furthermore, our bi-level perspective suggests new settings to study domain reweighting such as data-restricted scenarios and supervised fine-tuning, where optimized mixture ratios significantly improve the performance. Extensive experiments validate TANDEM's effectiveness in all scenarios.

</details>


### [309] [Low-Rank Decay for Grokking in Scale-Invariant Transformers: A Spectral-Geometric View](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04405)
*Mingyu Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a new regularizer, Low-Rank Decay (LRD), to address issues with standard weight decay in scale-invariant Transformer layers and demonstrates its effectiveness in promoting generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing normalization mechanisms in Transformers make weight decay insufficient for inducing generalization, as Frobenius-norm decay can only act radially and does not reshape the function of normalized layers.

Method: The authors propose Low-Rank Decay (LRD), a nuclear-norm-like spectral regularizer that acts tangentially in scale-invariant settings and continues compressing singular values, even after task gradients vanish.

Result: LRD induces fast effective-rank collapse in Query/Key matrices and improves the conditions for delayed generalization (grokking), verified on modular arithmetic tasks.

Conclusion: LRD breaks the limitations of L2 decay under scale invariance and introduces spectral-geometric benefits for learning and generalization in Transformer models.

Abstract: Modern Transformer architectures frequently employ normalization mechanisms such as RMSNorm and Query-Key Normalization, making parts of the model approximately scale-invariant with respect to weight magnitudes. In this regime, standard Frobenius-norm weight decay acts purely along the radial direction of the weight space and cannot directly simplify the function represented by the normalized layer. We study grokking in small algorithmic tasks through this lens and propose \emph{Low-Rank Decay} (LRD), a nuclear-norm-like spectral regularizer whose subgradient -- the polar factor $UV^\top$ -- retains a tangential component even in the scale-invariant setting. This distinction has a concrete dynamical consequence: after the model memorizes the training set and task gradients vanish, L2 decay can no longer reshape the weight spectrum, whereas LRD continues to compress singular values in an $\ell_1$-like fashion. On modular arithmetic tasks, we find that LRD induces rapid effective-rank collapse in Query/Key matrices and expands the data-fraction boundary at which delayed generalization (grokking) occurs. We further provide a spectral-geometric interpretation through the ``needle-to-fan'' expansion of the nuclear-norm subdifferential near low-rank strata.

</details>


### [310] [An Ensembled Latent Factor Model via Differential Evolution and Gradient Descent Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04408)
*Rui Zhang,Jinhang Liu,Wenbo Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces an ensemble latent factor model (ELFM-DEGDO) that combines gradient descent and differential evolution optimization methods for processing high-dimensional and incomplete data, outperforming traditional models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of existing latent factor models in handling heterogeneous, high-dimensional, and incomplete data using only gradient descent, which can yield insufficient and biased data representations.

Method: The proposed ELFM-DEGDO independently models data using two optimization methods—gradient descent and differential evolution—and combines their results using a self-adaptive weighting mechanism to leverage their complementary strengths.

Result: Experiments on three high-dimensional and incomplete datasets demonstrate that ELFM-DEGDO consistently outperforms several related latent factor models.

Conclusion: By combining gradient descent and differential evolution, the ELFM-DEGDO model produces more effective and unbiased representations for HDI data, making it a valuable improvement over existing methods.

Abstract: High-dimensional and incomplete (HDI) data are prevalent in many real-world big data scenarios. Latent factor models serve as a common representation learning approach, capable of uncovering informative latent factors from such data. Nevertheless, most existing latent factor models rely solely on gradient descent for optimization, which may lead to insufficient and biased representations, particularly when dealing with heterogeneous HDI data. Thus, this study proposes an Ensembled Latent Factor Model via Differential Evolution and Gradient Descent Optimization (ELFM-DEGDO) with two-fold designed: 1) two diverse latent factor models are independently modeled via differential evolution and gradient descent optimization, respectively, and 2) the two diverse latent factor models are combined via a customized self-adaptive weighting mechanism to effectively fuse their strengths. By leveraging the complementary advantages of both optimization paradigms, ELFM-DEGDO is able to produce more comprehensive and less biased representations for HDI data. Three HDI datasets are tested to show that ELFM-DEGDO consistently performs better than related several latent factor models.

</details>


### [311] [(Mis)generalization of Helpful-only Fine-tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04413)
*Mohammad Omar Khursheed,Baram Sosis,Fabien Roger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper explores the shortcomings of helpful-only AI models and investigates methods to mitigate issues like misalignment, refusal behaviors, and poor steerability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and address the generalization issues in helpful-only AI models, which are valuable for tasks requiring strict adherence to user intent.

Method: Analyzed existing helpful-only models to identify shortcomings, and experimented with synthetic document fine-tuning and character-related question training to address these issues.

Result: The paper finds that helpful-only models have emergent misalignment, refusal tendencies, and other deficiencies but demonstrates that training interventions can mitigate these problems.

Conclusion: Helpful-only models can be improved by targeted interventions such as synthetic document fine-tuning, enabling a more reliable adherence to user intent in AI systems.

Abstract: Helpful-only models, that is, models that are trained to always follow user intent, are valuable for dangerous capability evaluations and other areas of AI R&D where refusals would be an obstacle. Little is known about the generalization properties of helpful-only training: helpful-only models refuse less than their harmless counterparts, but previous work has not studied other dimensions of their alignment. We study the shortcomings of existing helpful-only models. We find that some show emergent misalignment, others have residual refusal behaviors, and most show poor steerability, sycophancy, and incoherent character. We show that simple anti-refusal training can cause many of these issues. None of these problems are necessary consequences of helpful-only training, though: we show that synthetic document fine-tuning and adding character-related questions to SFT and RL can mitigate them.

</details>


### [312] [Loss-Conditional PINNs for Parametric PDE Families](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04420)
*Anna Lazareva,Alexander Tarakanov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) often face challenges with loss weight selection. LC-PINN, introduced here, explores the entire weight space, learning a continuous family of solutions in one model that outperforms or matches traditional approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study addresses the challenge of loss weight selection in PINNs, which affects their ability to satisfy multiple physical constraints during optimization. Current methods rely on selecting a single good set of weights, which limits the training's robustness and versatility.

Method: The authors propose LC-PINN, an approach where loss weights or physical coefficients are treated as network inputs and sampled from a prior during optimization. This method enables training over a parametric family of solutions, using techniques like concatenation, FiLM architectures, and L-BFGS finishing for specific cases.

Result: LC-PINN demonstrates success on multiple parametric PDE problems, such as Helmholtz and Schrodinger equations. It matches or exceeds the performance of traditional, retrained PINNs while encompassing the entire solution family in a single model.

Conclusion: LC-PINN unifies loss-weight tuning and parameterized solution spaces into a single framework while maintaining computational efficiency. This improves PINN robustness, combining elements from classical PINNs and operator learning.

Abstract: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) approximate solutions of ODEs and PDEs by minimising a weighted combination of residual, boundary, initial, and data losses. Their performance is often dominated by the choice of loss weights: a poor weighting can drive training to a degenerate solution in which one physical constraint is satisfied while another is ignored. Existing methods select or adapt a single good set of weights. We take a different view: instead of tuning one weight vector, we explore the entire weight space during training.
  We introduce LC-PINN, which adapts the loss-conditional training of Dosovitskiy and Djolonga (2020) to the PDE-residual setting: the conditioning vector (either the loss weights or a scalar physical coefficient) is treated as a network input and sampled from a simple prior at every optimisation step. This turns PINN training into learning a continuous family of solutions indexed by that vector, with no solver-generated paired data. LC-PINN thus lies between classical PINNs and operator learning: it stays fully physics-informed but amortises training over a parametric family. Our contribution is not the loss-conditional construction itself, but its extension to PINNs, the unification of the loss-weight and parametric-coefficient regimes under one architecture (concatenation for loss weights, FiLM for coefficients), and a fixed-quadrature L-BFGS finishing protocol that makes the parametric-coefficient regime trainable.
  We give a lambda-invariance result for the conditional optimum and study LC-PINN on parametric Helmholtz, Schrodinger, viscous Burgers, and Buckley-Leverett equations. A single LC-PINN matches or improves retrained per-weight PINN baselines while parameterising the full family in one model, at a total cost that amortises favourably against per-instance retraining.

</details>


### [313] [LoopMoE: Unifying Iterative Computation with Mixture-of-Experts for Language Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04438)
*Wenkai Chen,Tianshu Li,Wenyong Huang,Yichun Yin,Lifeng Shang,Chengwei Qin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LoopMoE introduces a new looped sparse architecture in language models, addressing parameter capacity and computation depth while outperforming standard MoE models at similar scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To combine sparse routing and iterative computation in language models, achieving better Parameter efficiency and performance in resource-constrained environments.

Method: Introduced the LoopMoE model, featuring IterAdaLN for symmetry resolution in weight sharing and a capacity-balancing strategy to maintain the right ratios of active parameters.

Result: LoopMoE demonstrated consistent performance improvements over Vanilla MoE across multiple benchmarks, with gains observed both at 3B and 9B model scales.

Conclusion: The proposed architecture successfully integrates sparsity and iterative design, showing promise for advancing future looped language model development.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) and looped architectures scale models along two orthogonal axes, namely parameter capacity and effective depth. However, mainstream looped architectures rely on dense backbones that couple parameter count with per-token FLOPs, which makes it impossible to isolate the effect of iterative computation under matched budgets. To this end, we present LoopMoE, a looped MoE language model that integrates sparse routing with iterative weight-shared computation through two designs. The first is IterAdaLN, which resolves weight-sharing symmetry via a modulation signal jointly conditioned on the iteration index and the per-token hidden state. The second is a capacity-balancing strategy that recovers the attention-to-FFN active parameter ratio of well-tuned non-looped references. Together, these designs enable the first strictly controlled, head-to-head evaluation of a looped MoE against a Vanilla MoE under identical total parameters, per-token FLOPs, and active sublayer ratios. At the 3B scale, LoopMoE outperforms the Vanilla MoE on 8 of 9 downstream benchmarks with an average improvement exceeding 1 point. At the 9B scale, LoopMoE continues to outperform the matched Vanilla MoE, indicating that the architectural gain persists at larger scale. Our work establishes a controlled synthesis of sparsity and recurrence, and suggests a promising direction for looped language models.

</details>


### [314] [RowNet: A Memory Transformer for Tabular Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04445)
*Askat Rakhymbekov,Gulshat Muhametjanova*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces RowNet, a novel retrieval-based neural network designed for real estate valuation tasks, focusing on leveraging pairwise similarity features for robust predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address challenges in real estate valuation, such as heterogeneous features, sparse regional effects, and nonlinear interactions, which are inadequately handled by standard methods like multilayer perceptrons and gradient-boosted decision trees.

Method: The proposed RowNet uses a retrieval-based architecture that constructs pairwise similarity features against a memory bank of labeled properties. It has multiple layers including a retrieval layer for coarse target estimation and another for enhanced comparisons using learned attention heads. It also employs a mixture-of-experts module for final prediction.

Result: RowNet achieves improved accuracy in predicting real estate prices by effectively modeling the retrieval of similar properties and addressing property-specific challenges.

Conclusion: RowNet demonstrates a novel approach to property valuation by explicitly integrating retrieval mechanisms and diverse regularization techniques to improve regression performance over traditional models.

Abstract: Real estate valuation is a structured regression problem in which prices are governed by heterogeneous feature types, sparse regional effects, nonlinear interactions, and the practical logic of comparable properties. Standard multilayer perceptrons treat each row as an isolated vector and must learn locality, scale sensitivity, and categorical matching from supervision alone. Gradient-boosted decision trees provide strong tabular baselines, but their feature-centric splitting mechanism does not explicitly model the retrieval of similar historical observations.
  This paper presents RowNet, a retrieval-based neural architecture for real estate price-per-square-meter prediction. RowNet represents a query property through pairwise similarity features against a memory bank of labeled properties. A first retrieval layer estimates a coarse target from feature-only similarities. A second layer augments the memory comparison with target-consistency features and uses multiple learned attention heads to retrieve complementary comparable sets. A final mixture-of-experts module combines learned gating, residual correction, entropy regularization, and head-diversity regularization to produce the prediction.

</details>


### [315] [On Out-of-sample Embedding in UMAP](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04451)
*Mohammad Tariqul Islam,Jason W. Fleischer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper addresses the challenge of properly embedding out-of-sample points in UMAP while proposing a parameterized approach to improve the results, especially for complex data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: UMAP, a popular neighbor embedding algorithm, struggles with positioning new points in existing mappings, leading to the ‘repulsion effect,’ where points are misplaced on the boundaries instead of near their correlated clusters.

Method: The authors optimize the pairwise interactions within the k-nearest-neighbor graph and propose a parameterized UMAP approach to achieve better embeddings.

Result: Parameterizing UMAP improves embeddings compared to non-parametric models, particularly for complex datasets like medical images, and reduces the repulsion effect for out-of-sample points.

Conclusion: Parameterizing UMAP enhances its performance, particularly in complex data scenarios, and naturally mitigates the repulsion effect, improving trustworthiness and nearest neighbor classification results.

Abstract: Neighbor embedding algorithms reveal correlations in high-dimensional data by constructing an equivalent graph representation in a lower-dimensional space. An increasingly popular algorithm is Uniform Manifold Learning and Projection (UMAP), which uses algebraic topology to map distances between the two spaces. While it works well on many types of data sets, UMAP has trouble adding out-of-sample points to a pre-existing mapping. In particular, UMAP often places new points on the periphery of the found clusters, rather than in their interiors with their correlated neighbors. Here, we overcome this ``repulsion effect'' by optimizing pairwise interactions within the original k-nearest-neighbor graph. Moreover, we show that parameterizing UMAP obtains better embeddings than non-parametric algorithms, particularly as the data gets more complex (e.g., medical images). We also show that the repulsion effect is naturally mitigated when a parameterized UMAP is employed to embed the data. We characterize different UMAP approaches using trustworthiness, nearest neighbor classifiers, and by analyzing attractive and repulsive forces in the embeddings.

</details>


### [316] [ChessMimic: Per-Rating Transformer Models for Human Move, Clock, and Outcome Prediction in Online Blitz Chess](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04473)
*Thomas Johnson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces ChessMimic, a model system for chess prediction tasks using encoder-only transformers trained per 100-Elo rating band.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the prediction accuracy for human chess moves, game outcomes, and thinking times, tailored to varying skill levels.

Method: Utilized small encoder-only transformers conditioned on chess position, move history, player rating, and clock state; separate models trained per 100-Elo band.

Result: ChessMimic's move prediction surpasses Maia-2 across Elo bands and achieves comparable accuracy to Maia-3 models. Outcome prediction achieves 0.78 AUC, surpassing baseline models. Clock model provides usable predictions but is non-SOTA.

Conclusion: ChessMimic demonstrates promising accuracy and tailored calibration across skill bands, providing advancements in chess move, outcome, and thinking time predictions.

Abstract: We present ChessMimic, a system of three small encoder-only transformers - for move, thinking-time, and outcome prediction - conditioned on the position, recent move history, player rating, and clock state. We fit a separate instance of each model per 100-Elo rating band, trading parameter efficiency for sharper per-skill calibration. On a held-out month-wide slice of Lichess Rated Blitz games ChessMimic's human move prediction accuracy outperforms Maia-2 in every Elo band. Compared to Maia-3, our 9M parameter model's accuracy sits between Maia-3-5M and Maia-3-23M without the additional complexity of Geometric Attention Bias. In addition to the move matching model, we also train a game outcome model that conditions not only on the position, but also player ratings, time control, and remaining clock times. The outcome model achieves an AUC of 0.78 out of sample, beating Maia-2 as well as logistic regressions based on material, ratings, and clock time. Finally, we train a clock model that predicts human thinking times. The clock model provides a usable but non-SOTA per-ply think-time signal under ALLIE-style filters (Pearson r = 0.41, Spearman rho = 0.50, MAE 4.10 s, against ALLIE's reported r = 0.70), with the residual gap concentrated in per-position bucket sharpness rather than bucket-marginal calibration. A public demo is at 1e4.ai and we release code, per-band weights, and the C++ data-filter pipeline code in GitHub.

</details>


### [317] [LimiX-2M: Mitigating Low-Rank Collapse and Attention Bottlenecks in Tabular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04485)
*Yuanrui Wang,Xingxuan Zhang,Han Yu,Mingchao Ming,Gang Ren,Hao Yuan,Li Mao,Yunjia Zhang,Chun Yuan,Peng Cui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces the RaBEL tokenize-and-route framework to improve tabular foundation models (TFMs) by enhancing value sensitivity and improving computation alignment, demonstrated through the effective and efficient LimiX-2M model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address inefficiencies in TFMs caused by weak early-layer sensitivity to feature variations and redundant internal states, impacting both performance and computational efficiency.

Method: The authors propose the RaBEL framework, which includes expanding scalar features into localized RBF features (optionally exponent-gated) and reorganizing computational blocks to improve alignment with the readout process.

Result: The 2M-parameter LimiX-2M model based on RaBEL outperformed larger baseline models like TabPFN-v2 and TabICL on popular tabular benchmarks, while reducing training and inference costs.

Conclusion: Value-aware tokenization and readout-aligned computation can significantly improve the trade-off between accuracy and efficiency in TFMs, as demonstrated by the LimiX-2M model.

Abstract: Tabular foundation models (TFMs) increasingly rival tree ensembles, but their performance is often compute-inefficient: with standard affine scalar tokenization, each feature injects value variation through an essentially one-dimensional channel, and feature IDs/positional signals cannot increase within-feature value degrees of freedom, yielding weak early-layer value sensitivity and redundant hidden states. We present a unified \emph{tokenize-and-route} framework for strong TFMs: \textbf{RaBEL} expands each scalar into compact localized RBF features (optionally exponent-gated) to improve conditioning and shallow-layer effective rank, while a reordered bidirectional block \textbf{S$\rightarrow$N$\rightarrow$F} aligns computation with the readout by aggregating cross-sample context before feature mixing and using attention pooling. Together, these changes yield \textbf{LimiX-2M}, a 2M-parameter model that outperforms larger TabPFN-v2 and TabICL baselines on widely used tabular benchmarks while reducing training and inference costs. These results highlight value-aware tokenization and readout-aligned routing as key levers for improving the accuracy--efficiency trade-off in TFMs. Model checkpoints and inference code are available at https://github.com/limix-ldm-ai/LimiX.

</details>


### [318] [Episodic Memory Temporal Consistency for Cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04492)
*Zicheng Zhao,Yu Lan,Chengzhengxu Li,Zhaohan Zhang,Xiaoming Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes EMTC, an approach to improve cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning by addressing reward sparsity and exploration issues through temporally consistent episodic memory mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges of reward sparsity, exploration bottlenecks, local optima, and representation collapse in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL).

Method: EMTC, a framework with two components: (1) a Temporally Consistent Semantic Embedder combining contrastive learning and time-conditioned state reconstruction; and (2) a Temporal Consistency Gating Mechanism to filter ineffective rewards and avoid Q-value overestimation.

Result: EMTC demonstrates significant improvements in performance over state-of-the-art baselines. On SMAC and GRF benchmarks, it achieves up to 24% higher win-rates in super-hard scenarios and an average of 28% improvement across tasks.

Conclusion: EMTC addresses the limitations of existing episodic memory mechanisms in MARL, resulting in stronger performance and better trajectory optimization through effective memory retrieval and temporal consistency.

Abstract: Cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) frequently suffers from severe reward sparsity and exploration bottlenecks. While episodic memory mechanisms mitigate these issues by reusing high-return trajectories, they often trap agents in local optima due to unconstrained incentive distribution and semantic representation collapse. To address this, we propose Episodic Memory Temporal Consistency (EMTC), a framework that robustly constructs and selectively leverages historical experiences. EMTC introduces two synergistic components: (1) a Temporally Consistent Semantic Embedder that integrates contrastive learning with time-conditioned state reconstruction, preventing representation collapse and enabling precise memory retrieval; and (2) a Temporal Consistency Gating Mechanism that dynamically modulates episodic incentives based on temporal consistency error. This adaptive gate filters misleading signals from pseudo-successful trajectories, effectively mitigating Q-value overestimation. We provide theoretical guarantees, establishing a strict error bound that directly links the observable temporal consistency error to the underlying trajectory optimality and representation quality. Extensive evaluations on the SMAC and GRF benchmarks demonstrate that EMTC consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. Notably, compared to the strongest episodic baseline, EMTC achieves absolute win-rate improvements of up to 24% in super-hard SMAC scenarios and an average improvement of 28% across GRF tasks.

</details>


### [319] [Smart Picks in the Dark: Towards Efficient RLVR for Reasoning via Tracing Metacognitive Pivots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04503)
*Guangcheng Zhu,Shenzhi Yang,Haobo Wang,Xing Zheng,Yingfan MA,Xuening Feng,Zhongqi Chen,Bowen Song,Weiqiang Wang,Gang Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces PivotTrace, a framework enhancing data efficiency in RLVR by using attention dynamics to optimize annotation and training, achieving superior performance with fewer labeled samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address inefficiencies in Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) by enabling effective training with minimal labeled data under unsupervised conditions.

Method: The authors developed PivotTrace, which uses attention dynamics and metacognitive pivot tracing to quantify uncertainty. This allows for strategic selection of unlabeled data for annotation and training.

Result: PivotTrace demonstrated significant resource and performance efficiency by achieving better-than-supervised results with just 29.3% annotated data and 2.75 times faster convergence.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that PivotTrace’s approach to leveraging attention-based uncertainty estimation and adaptive data selection offers a novel pathway for efficient RLVR training.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has greatly advanced large reasoning models (LRMs), but it requires timely training on a huge fully-annotated dataset. To this end, data-efficient RLVR methods have been widely studied from two perspectives: (i) data selection methods identify a small subset of "golden" samples that yield near-full-data performance, but they rely on a pre-existing pool of labeled data. (ii) unsupervised RLVR methods train the model using its own internal supervision signals on large-scale unlabeled data, yet they exhibit suboptimal performance. Accordingly, we investigate the "pick in the dark" setup for RLVR, which aims to select, without prior supervision, unlabeled samples that are most beneficial for training and worthy of annotation. Through systematic analysis, we demonstrate that smart picks hinge on a well-calibrated uncertainty estimator to enable strategic partitioning of data for adaptive training regimes. Building on this insight, we propose PivotTrace, a three-way data triage framework that leverages attention dynamics to trace metacognitive pivots during reasoning. By precisely quantifying uncertainty through pivot density, PivotTrace achieves automated data routing to synergistically maximize both annotation and training efficiency. Empirically, PivotTrace surpasses the fully supervised LRM with only 29.3% annotated samples and 2.75 faster convergence.

</details>


### [320] [GeoMin: Data-Efficient Semi-Supervised RLVR via Geometric Distribution Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04516)
*Guangcheng Zhu,Shenzhi Yang,Haobo Wang,Xing Zheng,Yingfan MA,Xuening Feng,Zhongqi Chen,Kai Tang,Zhengqing Zang,Bowen Song,Weiqiang Wang,Gang Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes GeoMin, a method to improve semi-supervised RLVR by modeling feature distributions to overcome data-efficiency bottlenecks, surpassing baselines and even fully supervised models with minimal annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the inefficiencies in semi-supervised RLVR methods, which fail to fully utilize unlabeled data due to reliance on coarse performance heuristics.

Method: GeoMin is introduced to model global feature distributions on labeled data, enabling it to distinguish between correct and incorrect outputs and assess self-reward signals effectively.

Result: GeoMin improves performance by +4.1% compared to strong baselines and surpasses fully supervised approaches using only 10% of labeled data.

Conclusion: GeoMin demonstrates remarkable data efficiency and effectiveness, setting a new standard in semi-supervised RLVR while reducing reliance on expensive annotations.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) significantly advances LLM reasoning, yet it faces a dilemma: standard supervised scaling is throttled by high annotation costs, while unsupervised alternatives suffer from severe model collapse. Recent semi-supervised RLVR methods address this by using a small labeled set to guide unlabeled data, achieving a promising trade-off between training efficacy and annotation cost. However, they suffer from a severe data-efficiency bottleneck due to the reliance on coarse performance heuristics, leaving a vast majority of valuable instances underutilized. To this end, we propose GeoMin, which models global feature distributions on labeled data to decode the structural discrepancy between correct and incorrect rollouts, thereby establishing a robust prior to assess the reliability of self-reward signals and fully unleash the potential of unlabeled data. Empirically, GeoMin outperforms the strongest baselines by +4.1% and even surpasses fully supervised models with only 10% of the annotations, demonstrating remarkable data efficiency.

</details>


### [321] [Rollout-Level Advantage-Prioritized Experience Replay for GRPO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04560)
*Gyeongtae Yoo,Sanghyeok Park,Soohyuk Jang,Ik-hwan Kim,Sungroh Yoon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a rollout-level replay buffer for improving reinforcement learning efficiency when fine-tuning LLMs with GRPO, achieving notable performance and efficiency gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRPO is sample inefficient, as rollouts are discarded after one gradient update. Existing replay methods are unsuitable due to rapid policy drift in LLMs, causing stored rollouts to become stale and destabilize training.

Method: The paper introduces a rollout-level replay buffer for GRPO. It bounds staleness with age eviction (removing rollouts older than τ_max steps) and ensures on-policy data via fresh-anchored composition. Replay rollouts are independently selected based on priority (advantage magnitude) and staleness.

Result: The proposed method improves performance across three scales of Qwen3-Base models on five math benchmarks. Performance gains are positive at all scales and grow with model size, peaking at a +4.35 percentage point average improvement on the largest 4B model.

Conclusion: The rollout-level replay buffer makes reinforcement learning for fine-tuning LLMs more efficient by addressing staleness and data reuse issues in GRPO training, with significant accuracy and token efficiency improvements.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards with GRPO is a standard approach for post-training reasoning LLMs. It remains sample inefficient. Each rollout is used for a single gradient update and then discarded. Naive replay is not well suited in this setting because LLM policies drift quickly per gradient step. Stored rollouts therefore become stale and can destabilize training. We propose a rollout-level replay buffer for GRPO that stores and samples individual rollouts rather than whole groups. The buffer bounds staleness through age eviction. Any rollout older than tau_max training steps is removed. The buffer also preserves on-policy data via fresh-anchored composition. Each batch keeps its fresh on-policy rollouts and then concatenates replay rollouts drawn separately from the buffer. We prioritize replay by per-rollout advantage magnitude and recycle individual rollouts whose advantages are large. Across three Qwen3-Base scales on five math benchmarks, our method outperforms GRPO and naive replay baselines. Gains are positive at every scale and grow with model size. The largest gain is +4.35 pp on the five-benchmark average at 4B. Under an AES metric that jointly measures accuracy and token efficiency, the efficiency margin over GRPO is again largest at 4B, at +0.579.

</details>


### [322] [SurvPFN: Towards Foundation Models for Survival Predictions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04564)
*Samuel Böhm,Lennart Purucker,Frank Hutter,Pascal Schlosser*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces SurvPFN, a tabular foundation model (TFM) tailored for survival prediction tasks involving censored data, overcoming the limitations of standard TFMs in this context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the inability of standard tabular foundation models to handle censored data in survival prediction tasks, leading to biased and inaccurate real-world predictions.

Method: The proposed solution, SurvPFN, is a prior-data fitted network pretrained on millions of synthetic survival tasks. It models survival as distributional regression by generating data using Weibull event times, incorporating censored event indicators, and minimizing a censored negative log-likelihood.

Result: SurvPFN achieves competitive performance with classical and deep survival models on SurvSet, a collection of real-world survival tasks, without requiring dataset-specific tuning, specialized architectures, or feature engineering.

Conclusion: SurvPFN demonstrates that survival prediction can be approached as a continuous-time distributional regression problem with a censored loss, showcasing the potential of PFNs in survival analysis.

Abstract: Tabular foundation models (TFMs) have made rapid progress in standard classification and regression, but time-to-event survival prediction tasks have remained largely untouched. Unlike in standard regression tasks, survival prediction models must account for censored data. Standard TFMs cannot handle natively censored data, leading to biased and inaccurate predictions, making them unsuitable for real-world applications. To overcome this fundamental limitation, we propose \texttt{SurvPFN}, a prior-data fitted network (PFN), for survival prediction tasks. We pretrain \texttt{SurvPFN} on millions of synthetic survival prediction tasks to learn survival via distributional regression that accounts for censored data. \texttt{SurvPFN} works by (1) generating data with Weibull event times and a non-informative censoring mechanism; (2) integrating a censored event indicator; and (3) minimizing a censored negative log-likelihood. On SurvSet, a collection of real-world survival tasks, \texttt{SurvPFN} is highly competitive with classical and deep survival baselines without per-dataset fitting, a survival-specific architecture, or feature engineering. We show that survival can be treated as a continuous-time distributional regression problem with censored loss, unlocking the power of PFNs for time-to-event predictions.

</details>


### [323] [HalfNet: Randomized Neural Networks with Learned Subspace Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04583)
*Ethem Alpaydin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HalfNet uses random weights drawn from a low-rank factorized distribution that is learned from data, achieving performance comparable to fully trained neural networks with fewer parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the geometry of neural network weight distribution and reduce parameter reliance while maintaining predictive capabilities.

Method: Introduced HalfNet, which employs weights drawn from a data-learned, low-rank factorized distribution, and analyzed its performance on MNIST and CIFAR-10.

Result: HalfNet achieved accuracy comparable to trained MLPs with fewer parameters; demonstrated that the geometry of weight space is crucial, and accuracy is rank-dependent.

Conclusion: HalfNet highlights the importance of weight space geometry in neural networks and provides a data-driven embedding approach that aligns with various machine learning perspectives.

Abstract: Many researchers investigated neural networks with some of their weights fixed to values randomly drawn from a given distribution, e.g., $N(0, I)$. Our proposed HalfNet draws random weights from $N(0, Σ)$, where $Σ$, which defines the geometry of the distribution, has a low-rank factorization that we learn from data. Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 demonstrate that HalfNet can match the performance of fully trained multilayer perceptrons while using substantially fewer parameters. Spectral analysis indicates that much of the predictive power of neural networks lies in the geometry of their weight space rather than in the precise values of individual parameters, and we observe that accuracy scales smoothly with rank. HalfNet is not a neural architecture trick for low-rank structure; it implements a data-dependent random embedding that can also be interpreted through supervised metric learning, or random-feature and kernel perspectives.

</details>


### [324] [QuBLAST: A Framework for Quantizing Large Language Models with Block-Level Compression Approach and Activation Scaling Strategy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04620)
*Pasindu Wickramasinghe,Achyuta Muthuvelan,Rachmad Vidya Wicaksana Putra,Minghao Shao,Muhammad Shafique*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QuBLAST is a post-training quantization method for large language models (LLMs), reducing their size by 40%-45.2% with minimal performance degradation, leveraging block-level compression and activation scaling strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the high computational and memory costs of deploying LLMs on embedded systems, which are exacerbated by uniform post-training quantization and ineffective handling of activation outliers, especially for emerging non-conventional attention architectures.

Method: QuBLAST employs a novel block-level compression approach for mixed-precision quantization and an activation scaling strategy to mitigate the impact of activation outliers. Sensitivity of attention blocks is analyzed via cross-entropy loss to determine appropriate quantization levels.

Result: QuBLAST reduces the model size by 40%-45.2% while maintaining performance within a 5% perplexity increase on WikiText-2 and WikiText-103 datasets.

Conclusion: QuBLAST effectively improves memory and computational efficiency of LLMs, providing a viable solution for deploying these models on resource-constrained systems without significant performance trade-offs.

Abstract: LLMs have become the state-of-the-art algorithms for solving NLP tasks. However, they typically come at huge computational and memory costs, thus making them difficult to deploy on embedded systems. Toward this, state-of-the-art methods typically employ uniform post-training quantization (PTQ) across attention blocks of the network, hence overlooking the potential of applying different quantization levels in the same network. They also employ complex operations to mitigate the negative impact of activation outliers, hence incurring high computational overheads. Moreover, they have not considered evaluation using emerging LLMs with non-conventional attention architectures (e.g., state-space models), which pose different challenges in applying quantization. To address these limitations, we propose QuBLAST, a novel PTQ methodology that employs block-level compression approach with activation scaling strategy for LLMs. Block-level compression approach enables mixed-precision quantization across blocks of the network, while activation scaling strategy efficiently mitigates the negative impact of activation outliers. Specifically, QuBLAST first analyzes the sensitivity of different attention blocks in the pre-trained model through the cross-entropy loss analysis. QuBLAST leverages this sensitivity analysis to determine the weight quantization level for each attention block in the model. Furthermore, QuBLAST employs the activation scaling map for each block to control the range of activation values and mitigate the negative impact of activation outliers, thereby enabling better quantization results. Experimental results show that, QuBLAST reduces model sizes by 40%-45.2% across different model architectures (i.e., Qwen3-8B, Llama3-8B, Mistral v0.1-8B, and Falcon H1R-7B), while maintaining the performance within 5% perplexity increase for the WikiText-2 and WikiText-103 datasets.

</details>


### [325] [Learning symplectic model reduction based on a approximation theorem of symplectic embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04623)
*Liyi Feng,Yifa Tang,Yulin Xie,Ruili Zhang,Aiqing Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a Symplecticity-preserving Autoencoder (SpAE) to effectively address issues of model reduction in high-dimensional Hamiltonian systems, demonstrating its effectiveness through extensive experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the instability and failure of standard autoencoders in preserving symplectic structures, which are essential for accurate long-time predictions in high-dimensional Hamiltonian systems.

Method: The authors establish a universal approximation theorem for symplectic embeddings and design a new autoencoder architecture where the decoder is a symplectic embedding and the encoder is its symplectic projection, preserving symplecticity by construction.

Result: The proposed SpAE enhances both reconstruction and prediction accuracy while maintaining the symplectic structure. The method is validated through experiments on high-dimensional lattice and particle systems.

Conclusion: SpAE provides a powerful and theoretically sound framework for model reduction by maintaining the essential symplectic structure, thus improving prediction stability and accuracy in Hamiltonian systems.

Abstract: High-dimensional Hamiltonian systems play a central role in many scientific and engineering disciplines, with dynamics evolving on symplectic manifolds. Although deep learning provides powerful tools for constructing low-dimensional surrogates from data, the intrinsic symplectic structure is easily destroyed during model reduction. As a result, a standard autoencoder may produce latent coordinates that do not support a Hamiltonian flow, leading to unstable long-time prediction. In this paper, we first establish a universal approximation theorem for symplectic embeddings. Based on this theory, we propose symplecticity-preserving autoencoders (SpAE), in which the decoder is parameterized as a symplectic embedding and the encoder is constructed as the corresponding symplectic projection. This architecture is expressive enough to approximate nonlinear symplectic embeddings and the associated symplectic projections, preserves the symplectic structure exactly by construction, and can be trained by standard unconstrained optimization, thereby improving both reconstruction and prediction accuracy. Extensive experiments on high-dimensional lattice and particle systems demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

</details>


### [326] [VentAgent: When LLMs Learn to Breathe -- Multi-Objective Arbitration for ARDS Ventilation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04632)
*Teqi Hao,Yuxuan Fu,Xiaoyu Tan,Shaojie Shi,Bohao Lv,Yinghui Xu,Xihe Qiu*

Main category: cs.LG
